O R D E R
WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) No. 472/2021
1. It is stated that the present matter has been rendered
infructuous. The same stands disposed of, as infructuous.
2. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand closed.
WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) No. 497/2021
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner(s) prays for liberty to
withdraw the present Writ Petition. The same stands dismissed as
withdrawn.
2

--- Page 22 ---
Rest of the matters
4. Application(s) for permission to file SLP(s) is allowed.  IA
No. 60038/2021 (Application for permission to file SLP without
certified/plain copy of impugned order) is allowed.
 
5. Leave granted.
6. Varied issues regarding gross-irregularities in the matter of
distribution of essential supplies and services during  COVID-19
pandemic have been raised and considered in the lead matter as well
as connected petitions. Some matters also pertain to the grant of
compensation and/or refund of the expenditure incurred on treatment
from a private hospital, over and above the limit fixed by this
Court.
7. In our considered opinion, these independent issues cannot be
adjudicated in the instant proceedings. The larger issues, namely,
the challenges which the nation suddenly faced during the COVID-19
Pandemic were effectively dealt with by this Court at the relevant
time through various orders passed from time to time. The situation
being no longer in existence, these issues have become infructuous.
The instant proceedings are, accordingly, disposed of, with liberty
to each and every individual to raise their respective claim before
appropriate forum. It goes without saying that if the parties
approach the relevant hospital, they shall determine the claim, if
any, in accordance with law.
3

--- Page 23 ---
8. We make it clear that we have not expressed any opinion on
merits of the individual claim.
9. Pending application(s), if any, including application(s) for
intervention/impleadment stand closed.
..........................CJI.
       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
       (JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
..........................J.
       (VIPUL M. PANCHOLI) 
NEW DELHI;
MAY 19, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
ITEM NO. 302.1 CONMT.PET.(C) No. 877/2025 in C.A. No. 14604/2024
1. The matter was taken up today for further hearing.
2. In pursuance of our order dated 09.04.2026, a report has
been filed by the U.P.Awas Evam Vikas Parishad in the form of an
affidavit duly affirmed by Mr. P. Guruprasad, Chairman of the
Parishad.
5

--- Page 44 ---
 
3. In the report, the deponent has highlighted the steps that
have been taken so far to demolish all unauthorised construction
as directed by this Court.
4. We looked into the report threadbare. We also heard the
learned counsel appearing for the parties.
5. We take notice of the fact as stated in the report itself
that the 44 properties, which were identified earlier in which
various commercial activities were undertaken illegally, have
been sealed.
6. It is not in dispute that all these 44 properties were
originally meant for residential purpose. In fact, the original
plans which were sanctioned were also for residential purpose.
The permission was also granted for residential purpose.  Over a
period of time, people started utilising all these structures
for running a School, Maternity Clinics, Hospitals, Diagnostic
Centres, Banks, etc.
7. We are informed that most of the 44 properties are not in
the form of residential houses. They are all in the form of
commercial structures. If they are in the form of commercial
structures, we direct that these structures shall be demolished
at the earliest.  If there is any structure in the form of a
6

--- Page 45 ---
 
house, and if there is any additional construction put up in
that structure and utilised for the purpose of commercial use,
then the authority shall identify that part of the structure and
shall issue notice to the owner to demolish it within a period
of 15 days from the date of the notice.  If the owner on its own
demolishes it, then well and good; otherwise the authority shall
proceed to demolish that part of the unauthorised structure and
recover all the expenses that may be incurred for the purpose of
demolition.  The expenses shall be recovered as arrears towards
land revenue. 
8. We direct the authorities to start taking action in the
aforesaid regard at the earliest.  
9. We also looked into the Status Report insofar as demolition
of construction in the setback areas in 128 properties is
concerned.
10. With regard to setback areas, manifold contentions were
sought to be raised today including placing reliance on the
bylaws, etc. We direct that all setback areas of each and every
property shall be demolished.  
11. Mr. Pahwa, the learned senior counsel appearing for the
7

--- Page 46 ---
 
Parishad made a fervent appeal that there are some residential
houses falling within the category of EWS, where occupants have
put up unauthorised construction in the form of an additional
floor etc., may be permitted to be compounded.
12. In this regard, he invited our attention to Regulation 16
of the U.P. Housing and Development Board Regulations, 1982 (for
short, “the Regulations”). Regulation 16 reads thus:-
“16.  Compounding  of  unauthorised  construction:
(1) The authority can for reasons to be recorded
refuse  to  compound  any  case  of  unauthorised
construction.
(2)  The  following  category  of  unauthorised
construction  shall
not be compounded. 
(a) Any construction not in conformity of the land
use proposal contained in the Master Plan of the
area.
(b) On Government or public or Parishad land.
(c) On the first floor of construction within the
front,  side  and  rear  set  back  which  has  been
compounded earlier.
16.1 The fees for compounding shall be charged as
given in Appendix-1 or as revised by the Board in
future.
16.2  The  compounding  of  any  unauthorised
construction  can  be  done  only  on  the  written
request of the applicant."
8

--- Page 47 ---
 
13. The plain reading of Regulation 16(1)(a) referred to above
itself makes it explicitly clear that there shall not be any
compounding of unauthorised construction not in conformity of
the land use proposal contained in the Master Plan of the area.
14. There is no question of compounding in the aforesaid view
of the matter. We make it abundantly clear. 
15. We also heard Mr. P. Guruprasad, Chairman of the Parishad,
who has joined online.  We enquired with Mr. Guru Prasad whether
he has looked into or inquired about unauthorised constructions
and illegal use of residential premises in other parts of the
town. There could be many areas where people may be using
residential premises for commercial purpose, etc.
16.  We want the Chairman, to undertake a fresh exercise in the
aforesaid regard at the earliest, and file his report by the
next date of hearing. Teams shall be deputed to undertake
extensive inquiry and inspection in this regard. We say so
because there should not be any discrimination insofar as the
action which has been taken to demolish all illegal buildings. 
17. We also want Mr. Guru Prasad, the Chairman of the Parishad
9

--- Page 48 ---
 
to enquire whether any construction has come up illegally of any
residential houses/Kacchha Houses at the outskirts of the town
of Meerut more particularly at the place called Uldepur. The
learned counsel appearing for the petitioner shall also enquire
and assist us in this regard. 
18. We want a report in this regard also.
19. When we heard this matter for the first time and passed the
order, we had said that this litigation is an eye-opener not
only for the town of Meerut or the State of Uttar Pradesh, but
for the entire Country.  Over a period of last three months,
there have been some heart-wrenching casualties wherein innocent
children young and aged got charred to death because of fire. 
20. We want the authorities to ensure that such incidents do
not occur, and all steps are taken to protect human life.  We
once again impress upon the State and all its authorities that
if action is not taken today, it will be too late in the day
thereafter to proceed in accordance with law.  Do not give an
impression to the people at large that they will be saved or
their illegal constructions will be compounded on mere asking or
a mere request. Right action at the right time must be taken,
10

--- Page 49 ---
 
and that is the only way that the State will be able to maintain
the Rule of Law.
21. We make it clear for one and all that mere passage of time
or laxity on the part of the authorities in taking timely action
will not cure the illegality by itself. Passage of time can
never be a ground to compound the illegality or unauthorised
construction. What is illegal or what is unauthorised would
always remain so and by efflux of time would not become
authorised  or  is  liable  to  be  compounded.  This  idea  of
compounding which has come in the mind of the Parishad is
strongly deprecated. 
22. No man is above the law, and no man is below it; nor do we
ask any man’s permission when we ask him to obey it. Obedience
to the law is demanded as a right; not asked as a favor. We are
guardians of rights, and we have to tell people things they
often do not like to hear. 
23. Post this matter for further hearing on 21.9.2026 at 2.00
P.M.
24. On the next date of hearing, Mr. Pahwa, the learned senior
counsel appearing for Parishad, shall file a fresh report of
11

--- Page 50 ---
 
further developments that may have taken place. 
25. The affidavit filed on behalf of the Parishad is ordered to
be taken on record.
26. Mr. P. Guruprasad, the Chairman of the Parishad shall
appear on the next date of hearing through Video Conferencing.
27. At one point of time, the learned counsel argued that, in
fact there is no requirement of a setback, we outright reject
this argument.
Item No.302.2 CONMT.PET.(C) No. 851/2025 in C.A. No. 14604/2024 
28. None present on behalf of the State of Haryana.  
29. There is no report filed.  We want an exhaustive report by
the next date of hearing.
   List the CONMT.PET.(C) Nos. 877/2025, 851/2025 and MA Diary
No. 7071 of 2026 in 14604/2024 on 21.09.2026 at 2.OO P.M.
 
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)

W.P. (C) NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
1 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION 
WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 762 OF 2026 
BANASTHALI VIDYAPITH      …PETITIONER(S) 
VERSUS 
NATIONAL COUNCIL FOR  
TEACHER EDUCATIONS    …RESPONDENT(S) 
 
WITH 
 
WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 768 OF 2026 
 
 O R D E R  
 
 
1. The present writ petitions under Article 32 of the Constitution of 
India have been filed by Banasthali Vidyapith and I.P. (P.G.) 
College, Campus -II, Bulandshahr , respectively. The petitioners 
seek, inter alia, modification of the recognition granted by the 
National Council for Teacher Education (hereinafter referred to 
as “NCTE”) for conducting the Integrated Teacher Education 
Programme (hereinafter referred to as “ITEP”), so as to  make 
such recognition operative from the academic session 2026 -27 
instead of the academic session 2027 -28. In Writ Petition (Civil) 
No. 768 of 2026, the petitioner has also sought a consequential 
direction to Chaudhary Charan Singh University, Meerut 
(hereinafter referred to as “the CCS University”) to grant 

--- Page 64 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
2 
affiliation for conducting the recognised ITEP courses from the 
academic session 2026-27. 
 
2. Since the issues arising in the two writ petitions are connected 
and relate to the academic session from which the recognition 
granted by NCTE is to operate, they are being considered by this 
common order. 
 
3. The factual and procedural background leading to the present 
writ petitions is as follows:  
 
3.1. Pursuant to the public notices dated 15.03.2024 and 
22.04.2024 issued by NCTE, applications were invited for 
grant of recognition for conducting ITEP from the academic 
session 2025 -26. Both the petitioners submitted their 
respective applications in May 2024 and furnished the 
documents and compliances sought from them during the 
course of consideration of their applications. 
 
3.2. The application submitted by Banasthali Vidyapith was 
considered by the Western Regional Committee of NCTE 

--- Page 65 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
3 
(hereinafter referred to as “WRC”). After scrutiny of the 
application and inspection by the visiting team, a Letter of 
Intent was issued on 24.10.2025. The petitioner submitted 
its compliance on 22.12.2025. The application was 
thereafter refused by order dated 05.02.2026. The appeal 
preferred by the petitioner was allowed on 26.02.2026 and 
the matter was remanded to the WRC for reconsideration. 
Upon reconsideration, the WRC granted recognition by 
order dated 21.05.2026 for conducting four units of ITEP 
with a total intake of 200 students from the academic 
session 2027-28.  
 
3.3. The application submitted by I.P. (P.G.) College, Campus-II, 
Bulandshahr was considered by the Northern Regional 
Committee of NCTE (hereinafter referred to as “NRC”). The 
application was initially refused by order dated 20.01.2025 
on the ground that the petitioner did not satisfy the 
prescribed shortlisting criteria. The appeal preferred by the 
petitioner was allowed by order dated 16.09.2025 and the 
matter was remanded to the NRC for fresh consideration. 
Thereafter, the NRC undertook further scrutiny, 

--- Page 66 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
4 
constituted a visiting team and issued a Letter of Intent on 
26.02.2026. Upon submission of the requisite compliance, 
recognition was granted by order dated 20.05.2026 for 
conducting one unit each of B.Sc. B.Ed. and B.Com. B.Ed., 
with a total intake of 100 students, from the academic 
session 2027 -28. Since the petitioner is affiliated to the 
CCS University, it has also sought grant of the requisite 
affiliation for conducting the aforesaid courses from the 
academic session 2026-27. 
 
3.4. Thus, although the petitioners had applied for recognition 
for the academic session 2025 -26, the recognition 
ultimately granted to them was made operative from the 
academic session 2027-28. The petitioners have, therefore, 
sought modification of the respective recognition orders so 
as to enable them to commence the recognised courses 
from the academic session 2026-27. 
 
4. The present writ petitions seek modification of the respective 
recognition orders so as to  make the recognition operative from 
the academic session 2026-27. In Writ Petition (Civil) No. 768 of 

--- Page 67 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
5 
2026, consequential relief has also been sought against the CCS 
University for grant of the requisite affiliation for the said 
academic session. The counselling process for admission to ITEP 
for the academic session 2026 -27 is yet to commence. The 
petitioners contend that, unless appropriate relief is granted at 
this stage, they would be unable to participate in the admission 
process and would lose the opportunity to commence the 
recognised courses during the academic session 2026-27. 
 
5. The writ petitions were taken up on 15.06.2026, when notice 
was issued and NCTE was granted time to file its response. On 
07.07.2026, a further period of one week was granted to NCTE 
to file its counter affidavits. NCTE has since filed its counter 
affidavits in both matters. We have heard the learned counsel 
appearing for the petitioners and the learned counsel appearing 
for NCTE. We have also considered the material placed on 
record and the counter affidavits filed on behalf of NCTE.   
 
6. It is not in dispute that the petitioners had submitted their 
respective applications in May 2024 for grant of recognition for 
the academic session 2025-26. After undertaking the process of 

--- Page 68 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
6 
scrutiny, inspection and consideration of the compliances 
furnished by the petitioners, the concerned Regional 
Committees granted recognition in May 2026. The recognition 
was, however, made operative only from the academic session 
2027-2028.  
 
7. Admittedly, the deficiencies pointed out to the petitioner -
institutions stand removed and the petitioners have fulfilled the 
applicable eligibility conditions. NCTE has already granted 
recognition to Banasthali Vidyapith by order dated 21.05.2026 
and to I.P. (P.G.) College, Campus -II, Bulandshahr  by order 
dated 20.05.2026, although such recognition has been made 
operative from the academic session 2027 -28. The limited issue 
which, therefore, remains for consideration relates to the 
academic session from which such recognition should operate.  
 
8. Having regard to the time taken in processing the applications 
and the fact that the counselling process for admission to ITEP 
for the academic session 2026 -27 is yet to commence, the 
petitioners would lose an entire academic session if appropriate 
relief is not granted at this stage. Such an eventuality would 

--- Page 69 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
7 
also defeat the relief sought in the present writ petitions. On the 
other hand, permitting the recognition to operate from the 
academic session 2026 -27 would not dilute the regulatory 
requirements prescribed by NCTE. Insofar as I.P. (P.G.) College, 
Campus-II, Bulandshahr is concerned, the commencement of 
the recognised courses would additionally remain subject to the 
grant of the requisite affiliation by the CCS University and 
fulfilment of all other applicable requirements. 
 
9. Considering that the petitioner -institutions have fulfilled the 
applicable eligibility conditions and have already been granted 
recognition, we are of the view that they ought to be 
provisionally permitted to participate in the counselling and 
admission process for ITEP for the academic session 2026 -27. 
Such participation shall remain subject to fulfilment of the 
applicable statutory and regulatory requirements. 
 
10. For the reasons stated above, the writ petitions are allowed in 
the following terms: 
 
 

--- Page 70 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
8 
For Writ Petition (Civil) No. 762 of 2026 
i) The National Council for Teacher Education shall issue a 
modified recognition order forthwith and in any case within 
week Vidyapith by order dated 21.05.2026 operative from 
the academic session 2026-27 and permitting the petitioner 
to admit students against the recognised intake. 
ii) Upon issuance of the modified recognition order, the 
petitioner shall be provisionally permitted to participate in 
the counselling and admission process for the academic 
session 2026-27, subject to compliance with the applicable 
norms and regulatory requirements. 
 
For Writ Petition (Civil) No. 768 of 2026 
i) The National Council for Teacher Education shall issue a 
modified recognition order forthwith and in any case within 
a week  from today, making the recognition granted to I.P. 
(P.G.) College, Campus -II, Bulandshahr by order dated 
20.05.2026 operative from the academic session 2026 -27 
and permitting the petitioner to admit students against the 
recognised intake. 
 

--- Page 71 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
9 
ii) The CCS University shall take all consequential steps and 
grant the requisite affiliation to the petitioner for conducting 
the recognised courses during the academic session 2026 -
27 immediately after modified recognition is issued and in 
any case within three days thereafter, subject to fulfilment 
of the applicable requirements. 
iii) Upon issuance of the modified recognition order and the 
requisite affiliation, the petitioner shall be provisionally 
permitted to participate in the counselling and admission 
process for the academic session 2026 -27 and admit 
students against the recognised intake. 
 
11. Since the counselling process for admission to ITEP for the 
academic session 2026 -27 is yet to commence, NCTE and the 
CCS University, wherever applicable, shall ensure that the 
petitioners are not excluded from the counselling and admission 
process merely on account of the academic session specified in 
the original recognition orders. 
 
12. The writ petitions are accordingly allowed and disposed of in the 
above terms. Pending application(s), including I.A. No. 178906 

--- Page 72 ---
 
 
W.P.(C)NO.762 OF 2026 & connected case 
10 
of 2026 in Writ Petition (Civil) No. 762 of 2026 and I.A. No. 
179682 of 2026 in Writ Petition (Civil) No. 768 of 2026, shall 
stand disposed of. 
 
…………………………………J. 
[VIKRAM NATH] 
 
 
 
…………………………………J. 
[SANDEEP MEHTA] 
 
NEW DELHI; 
JULY 14, 2026

CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
 (arising out of SLP(Crl.) No. 10983/2026)
ANIL KURRE APPELLANT(S)
               VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH RESPONDENT(S)
with
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(Crl.) No. 11386/2026)
O R D E R
Crl.A. @ SLP(Crl.) No. 10983/2026
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellant,  Anil  Kurre,  is
aggrieved by the denial of regular
bail by the High Court of Chhattisgarh
at  Bilaspur,  vide order  dated
06.02.2026  passed  in  MCRC  No.
381/2026. He seeks bail in relation to
Crl.A. @ SLP(Crl.) No. 10983/2026 etc. 1

--- Page 84 ---
Crime/First Information Report (FIR)
No.   763/2025,  dated  13.08.2025
registered  with  Police  Station  –
Balodabazar, District – Balodabazar-
Bhatapara,  Chhattisgarh,  for  the
offences  punishable  under  Sections
317(4), 318(4) and 61(2)(a) of the
Bharatiya  Nyaya  Sanhita,  2023  and
Section  66D  of  the  Information
Technology Act, 2000. 
3. The  appellant  has  been  in  custody
since  24.10.2025.   The  trial  has
commenced but only 6 out of the 19
listed witnesses are stated to have
been examined till date.
4. Given the nature of the allegations
levelled against the appellant which
are  yet  to  be  established  by  the
prosecution, we are of the opinion
that his continued incarceration is
not warranted at this stage. 
5. Accordingly,  we  accept  the  present
appeal and direct that the appellant,
Crl.A. @ SLP(Crl.) No. 10983/2026 etc. 2

--- Page 85 ---
Anil Kurre, shall be released on bail
in  connection  with  the  aforestated
Crime/FIR on such appropriate terms
and conditions as may be fixed by the
trial Court.
6. In addition thereto, the appellant,
Anil Kurre, shall cooperate during the
course of the trial and shall not
delay the proceedings therein.
7. The impugned order is set aside and
the  appeal  is  allowed  in  the
aforestated terms.
8. We clarify that we have not made any
observations/comments on the merits of
the case and any observation made in
this  order  is  meant  only  for  the
limited purpose of grant of bail.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.
Crl.A. @ SLP(Crl.) No. 10983/2026 etc. 3

 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 102 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal  No(s).        /2026
SLP (C) No(s). 25535/2025
VIDYASAGAR                                       Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
PRANAV PACHORI & ORS.                            Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 25.08.2025 passed
by the High Court 1 in First Appeal Defective No. 627/2025,
whereby the appeal preferred by the present appellant (i.e.,
maternal grandparent of the child) against the order of the
Family Court granting the custody of the child to his natural
father (i.e., the respondent herein”) was disposed of with
certain directions without interfering with the order of
transfer of custody.
4. The Ward whose custody is in issue is the natural son of
the respondent and, therefore, in law the respondent would be
his natural guardian. The issue raised by the appellant in
the proceedings before the Family Court as well as the High
Court was whether it would be appropriate to handover custody
1 1 The High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
1

--- Page 103 ---
of the child to such father who is an accused in the dowry
death of child’s mother. 
5. As by the date of the order passed by the High Court the
criminal trial was pending, on 07.10.2025, while entertaining
the Special Leave Petition, we issued notice to the first
respondent and stayed the effect and operation of the order
passed by the High Court. Thereafter, we modified the order
passed earlier by providing visitation right to the first
respondent. The said arrangement, as was directed vide order
dated 03.12.2025, continued with certain modifications.
6. Today, when the matter was taken up, we have been
informed that the first respondent, who was facing Sessions
Trial No. 1087/2022 on the charge of dowry death of his wife,
has been acquitted vide order dated 04.05.2026 passed by the
Court of Additional Sessions Judge (Fast Track Court – II),
Etah.  Certified copy of the order of the Trial Court has
been placed before us which is taken on record.
7. The learned Counsel for the appellant submits that though
the  first  respondent  has  been  acquitted  in  the  case
registered against him, an appeal has been preferred against
the order of acquittal which is yet to be decided. In
response,  the  learned  Counsel  for  the  first  respondent
submits that there is a presumption of innocence and that
presumption  is  fortified  by  the  acquittal  of  the  first
2

--- Page 104 ---
respondent and, therefore, mere pendency of the appeal cannot
cloud the right of a father to the custody of his minor son.
He has also pointed out that the trial court has taken note
of various facts and circumstances while writing the judgment
of acquittal and it had noted that death of the deceased did
not appear homicidal. Besides, the trial court has also
noticed the statement of PW1, brother of the appellant, that
there were money transfers in the account of the deceased
from her in-laws’ side.
8. Be that as it may, it would not be appropriate for us to
comment on the merits of the order of acquittal. However,
what cannot be denied is that there exists an order of
acquittal and this matter was entertained primarily on the
ground that there was a criminal case pending against the
first respondent on the date when the Family Court and the
High Court had passed the custody order. 
9. As on date, the first respondent stands acquitted and,
therefore, there would be a presumption of his innocence till
such time the order of acquittal is reversed.
10. At this stage, we were informed by the appellant that the
child  is  attending  a  play  school,  namely,  Bawanbari
International School.
11. The learned Counsel for the first respondent informed us
that the first respondent will also get the child admitted in
3

--- Page 105 ---
a school and he has already shortlisted St. Paul’s School for
the purpose, which, according to him, is a better School. 
12. At this stage, it was pointed out that the High Court,
while disposing of the appeal, had directed that the ward
shall continue his schooling from the school where he is
currently placed for a further period of two years. According
to the appellant, this direction was necessitated because if
the child’s custody were to be transferred and the school had
to be changed, it may have resulted in a traumatic experience
for the child. In that light, it has been submitted that if
this Court proposes to handover custody of the child to the
father, the school, where the child is currently studying,
should not be changed till the end of this academic session.
13. We find substance in the aforesaid submission.
14. At this stage, we inquired from the first respondent
whether  he  has  remarried  after  death  of  ward’s  natural
mother. The respondent candidly stated that so far he is
single, though he intends to get married.
15. Taking a clue from the above statement, the learned
Counsel  for  the  appellant  submits  that  this  Court  may
therefore leave it open for the appellant to seek custody of
the child in the event the child is not comfortable in the
company of his stepmother.
16. It is well-settled that the Court’s primary concern is
4

--- Page 106 ---
the welfare of the child. If the child’s welfare so demands,
the  Court  can  always  revisit  the  custody  order,  and
therefore, there can be no absolute finality attached to a
custody order. In such view of the matter, we need not
comment  on  whether  the  custody  of  the  child  should  be
transferred back to the grandparent of the child post second
marriage of the first respondent. We, therefore, leave it
open for the appellant to apply for the custody of the child
if the welfare of the child so demands in the changed
circumstances.
17. In light of the discussion above and having regard to the
fact that we had entertained this appeal primarily on the
ground that the first respondent was accused of the offence
of dowry death of his wife, which ground does not survive
post his acquittal, we deem it appropriate to dispose of this
appeal with the following directions:
(i) The appellant shall handover custody of the minor
child (Arya) to his natural father i.e., the
first respondent by 31st July 2026. To facilitate
handing over of the custody, the appellant shall
produce the child before the Principal Judge,
Family Court, Etah on 31 st July 2026 at 10.30
a.m. Upon such production, the custody of the
child  shall  be  transferred  to  the  first
5

--- Page 107 ---
respondent and a memorandum regarding transfer of
the custody shall be prepared and endorsed by the
concerned clerk of that Court;
(ii) The direction of the High Court in paragraph 7 of
its order, which is reproduced below:
“7. We  agree with  view taken  by the
Family  Court.  However,  we  deem  it
necessary to modify directions given in
impugned judgment, for appellant to meet
the boy once a week for two hours in
respondent's residence, failing which at
a  neutral  place.  The  modification  is
necessary  because  respondent  cannot
dispute that the boy has been well looked
after since his mother died and till
date. In the circumstances, the boy will
spend every alternate weekend with his
maternal  grandfather.  Respondent  will
drop him at his maternal grandfather's
house on the Friday evening and collect
him on following Sunday evening. We have
made queries to ascertain the boy is
presently  studying  in  Bawanbari
International School, Post Ekah, District
Firozabad.  On  query  we  are  told,
respondent  intends  to  shift  him  to
another school being St. Paul's Public
School,  Awagarh,  District  Etah.  On
further  query  Ms.  Singh  submits  on
instructions from her client present in
the Court, for two years from date his
client will not cause transfer of the boy
from his present school. We have required
this concession because the boy is being
removed  from  familiar  to  new
circumstances of residence. Change from
the  school  at  this  time,  which  is
accessible from the father's house, is
not  in  welfare  of  the  child.”
6

--- Page 108 ---
shall continue subject to the directions
herein below;
(iii)The stipulation in the High Court’s order that
School of the child shall not be changed for a
period of two years is modified and substituted by
the direction that the child’s School shall not be
changed till the end of this academic session (i.e.,
2026-2027); and
(iv) The child shall spend at least one week of the
summer  vacations  and  one  week  of  the  winter
vacations with the appellant in appellant’s own
house. The said week, falling in that vacation
period, shall be mutually decided by the parties
subject to the convenience of the child as well as
the parties.
  18. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  25-08-2025
in FAD No. 627/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
VIDYASAGAR                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRANAV PACHORI & ORS.                              Respondent(s)

IA No. 222275/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 222279/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE LENGTHY LIST OF DATES

signed order placed on the file.

(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
9

--- Page 111 ---
2026 INSC 714
Page 1 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
 
Reportable  
 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
Civil Appeal No.………....of 2026 
[@ Special Leave Petition (C) No.31063 of 2025] 
 
 
Videsh Lal and Anr. 
…Appellants 
Versus 
Lok Singh and Ors.                                             
…Respondents 
 
 
O R D E R 
 
 
Leave granted. 
2. Whether the death caused by a motor vehicle hitting a 
person who accidently fell on the road would constitute an 
insurable claim is the question raised herein.  Admittedly, a 19 
year old boy riding a bicycle with a pillion rider fell on the road 
when the cycle slipped.  The car, driven by the first respondent 
is alleged to have run over the boy, sn uffing his life out. The 
father approached the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal for 
compensation which was declined on the ground that the cycle 
rider was negligent; which was affirmed by the High Court.  

--- Page 112 ---
Page 2 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
3. Before us, learned counsel for the appellants pointed out 
that the question is whether the deceased accidently fell on the 
road but as to whether the motor vehicle was driven safely.  The 
driver should have applied his brakes and in that event the 
death could have been averted.  
4. On the other hand, learned counsel for the insurance 
company vehemently pointed out that both the father and the 
pillion rider examined as AW-1 and AW2 spoke in one voice 
that the boy fell on the road when the cycle slipped on the 
uneven terrain. Hence, there can be no negligence found on 
the driver of the vehicle.  It is also pointed out that in the 
criminal case filed against the first respondent he was 
acquitted.  There is hence no cause to allow the claim, is the 
argument.   
5. We have carefully perused the evidence on record as 
produced in the memorandum. AW-1 is the father, who was not 
present at the time of the accident.  Hence, there can be no 
credence placed on his version in the First Information 
Statement that the “cycle slipped and fell”. However, AW-2, an 
eyewitness who was also riding along with the deceased 

--- Page 113 ---
Page 3 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
speaks of the cycle having slipped and the car having not hit 
the cycle. We will take it that the fall on the road was not due to 
the motor vehicle accident but was purely accidental. 
However, the fact remains that the boy was run over by the car 
and he was taken to the hospital by the driver of the car.  
6. It is pertinent that the consistent stand of the driver of the 
car is that he was only a good samaritan and he took an injured 
lying on the road to the hospital who was also examined, which 
deposition is produced as Annexure P-10. The witness does not 
speak of the car being driven with due caution or that the boy 
was hit since he suddenly fell on the road and that the driver 
was taken unawares.  There is not even a suggestion made to 
the said witness by the insurer as to the car being driven safely 
by the insurer. 
7.   We have also looked at the judgment of the criminal court 
produced as Annexure R3/1 by the insure r, d espite the trite 
principle that in adjudicating a claim under the Motor Vehicles 
Act, 1988, the standard of proof of negligence is 
preponderance of probabilities while that in a criminal case is 
proof beyond reasonable doubt.  Be that as it may, we see from 

--- Page 114 ---
Page 4 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
the order produced that the criminal court had specifically 
noticed the deposition of the pillion rider that the car did not hit 
the cycle and the deceased fell on the road accidently when the 
cycle slipped. He deposed that the car was moving at a high 
speed but the Court found that he did not specifically speak of 
a rash and negligent driving. The seizure witnesses except that 
of the vehicle , as produced by the driver , turned hostile. The 
evidence led was held to have raised a reasonable doubt on 
which reasoning the accused was acquitted. This is not to say 
that the car was being driven safely and in normal speed, 
especially when the acquittal was by reason of conferring the 
benefit of doubt on the accused.  
8. We cannot but emphasize that the drivers of motor vehicles 
on roads have to maintain a higher standard of caution since 
many contingencies may arise when the vehicle would have to 
be stopped abruptly ; which would not be possible when the 
vehicle is driven rashly or negligently  and at times the speed 
itself brings in the aspect of negligence .  Contingencies like a 
pedestrian falling on the road or a child running across the road 
are possibilities which should require the drive r to be doubly 

--- Page 115 ---
Page 5 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
cautious, especially on highways when the traffic is light and 
there is free passage.  Lack of due caution will amount to 
negligence.   
9. The case of the driver, as we noticed, is that he was involved 
in a rescue operation which was not substantiated and hence 
cannot be believed. The police registered a case against the 
driver for rash and negligent driving. The acquittal by the 
criminal court was also conferring him the benefit of doubt. 
Neither the insurance company, nor the Tribunal or the owner 
of the vehicle a ttempted to prove in evidence that the injuries 
caused on the person , leading to death, were  caused only by 
an accidental fall and not on being hit by a motor vehicle.   
10.   The totality of the circumstances in this case persuades us 
to find negligence on the driver of the car who could have 
applied brakes and stopped the car on seeing a cyclist falling 
down; if the car was driven safely .  There is no case set up of 
the vision being hampered by extraneous circumstances or the 
car having come across the person lying on the road after 
negotiating a curve on the road.  It is rather clear that the driver 
of the car was negligent and did not apply brakes immediately 

--- Page 116 ---
Page 6 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
so as to avoid hitting the person lying on the road  or despite 
applying brakes the car could not be stopped because of the 
high speed in which it was driven; spoken of by the pillion 
rider. 
11.  We, hence, overturn the dismissal of the claim petition by 
the Tribunal as affirmed by the High Court.  
12.   Considering the long passage of time, we are of the 
opinion that compensation can be computed by this Court itself 
since otherwise it would lead to further delay.  The deceased 
was said to be a student who ha d passed his 11th higher 
secondary examination, the certificates of which are produced 
along with the memorandum and also before the Tribunal. The 
father deposed that he was earning Rs.6,000/- per month while 
working as a part time salesman in a medical shop. There is no 
cross examination on this count and more significantly the 
pillion rider too, a friend of the deceased, corroborated the part 
time employment of the deceased. True, the father also had a 
contention that the deceased aspired to become an agricultural 
officer to which there was no evidence produced.  In any case, 
we are of the opinion that the monthly income of the deceased 

--- Page 117 ---
Page 7 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
can be adopted at the rate of Rs.6,000/- as claimed by the father 
with enhancement for future prospects. The multiplier applied 
would be 18 since the deceased was only 19 years of age. One 
third of the above amount would have to be applied to the 
annual income for the personal expense since the parents of the 
deceased comprised a family of three. Loss of consortium has 
to be granted to both the father and the mother as also the 
claimants would be entitled to funeral expenses and loss of 
estate as laid down in National Insurance Company Ltd. v. 
Pranay Sethi1. The total amount towards compensation will be 
as under: - 
Sl. 
No. 
Compensation for Amount 
1. Loss of income 
(6000 x 140% x 12 x 1 8 x 
2/3) 
Rs.12,09,600/- 
2. Loss of consortium  Rs.80,000/- 
3. Loss of estate Rs.15,000/- 
4. Funeral expenses Rs.15,000/- 
 Total Rs.13,19,600/- 
 
1 (2017) 16 SCC 680 

--- Page 118 ---
Page 8 of 8 
CA @ SLP(C) No.31063 of 2025  
 
13.   The amount awarded shall be satisfied with 7% interest  
within a period of two months from the date of this order. 
14.   The appeal is allowed in the aforestated terms.  
15.   Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
.……….…….……….……. J. 
                                                    (N. KOTISWAR SINGH) 
 
 
 
 
.……….…….……….……. J. 
                                                  (K. VINOD CHANDRAN) 
NEW DELHI 
JULY 09, 2026. 

--- Page 119 ---

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

                                VERSUS

IA No. 135967/2026 - INTERVENTION APPLICATION
IA No. 148795/2026 - INTERVENTION APPLICATION
IA No. 26427/2026 - INTERVENTION/IMPLEADMENT
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
MR. SUDHIR KUMAR SAXENA, LD. SENIOR ADVOCATE TO ASSIST THE HON'BLE
COURT (VIDE ORDER DATED 09.03.2026) 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Saurabh Kirpal, Sr. Adv.                   
                   Ms. Sija Nair, Adv.
                   Mr. Anuj Kapoor, AOR
                   Ms. Madiah Shahjar, Adv.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Application (IA No. 135967/2026) for intervention is allowed.
The Foundation is permitted to assist the Court. The Foundation may
submit a proposal with respect to the actions that may require
judicial intervention by this Court. 
2. In deference to the previous orders, learned Solicitor General
of India has placed on record a notification dated 22.05.2026
issued by the Union of India, Ministry of Social Justice and
Empowerment  (Department  of  Empowerment  of  Persons  with
Disabilities), whereby the entry no. 1(A)(e) in the Schedule to the
Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 has been amended.
Accordingly, in the heading ‘Physical disability’ and sub-heading
‘Locomotor disability’, serial number (e) now reads as follows:
“(e) “acid attack victim” means a person disfigured
externally or internally, due to violent assault or
self-infliction  or  accident,  by  throwing,
administering, spilling of acid or similar corrosive
substance.”
 
3. Since the above-stated amendment is admittedly clarificatory
in nature, it shall be deemed to have been in existence with effect
from the date when the Rights of Persons with Disability Act, 2016
came into force. The necessary consequences shall follow.
7

--- Page 126 ---
4. With respect to the other issues, post the matter for further
consideration on 31.08.2026.

ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
8

--- Page 127 ---
1
   IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
     CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No.________ OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(Civil) No.23621/2023)
THE MARWARI PANCHAYAT, NAGAON (GOPAL GAUSHALA)   Appellant(s)
                                 VERSUS
KHAGORIJAN ANCHALIK KRISHAK         Respondent(s)
SURAKSHA SAMITI & ORS
       O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellant  would  call  in
question the order passed by the Division
Bench of the High Court dismissing the
Writ Appeal which in turn, was preferred
against the order passed by the learned
Single Judge allowing the Writ Petition
preferred by the respondent no.1 which
laid challenge to the State Government’s
communication dated 06.10.1970 by which,
the settlement of the subject land ad
measuring  75  bighas,  was  approved  in
favour of the appellant for establishing a
Gaushala.  The allotment/ settlement was
proposed by the Deputy commissioner on

--- Page 128 ---
2
11.09.1970  and  this  order  was  also
challenged  in  the  Writ  Petition.  The
subject land is situated in Majorati VGR
in  Nizsahar  Mouza,  District-Nagaon
(Assam).
3. Admittedly, the Writ Petition was
preferred in the year 2011, i.e. after 41
years of the allotment vide communication
dated  11.09.1970  issued  by  the  Deputy
Commissioner, and subsequently approved by
the State Government vide communication
dated 06.10.1970.
4. The only ground explaining delay in
filing the Writ Petition was that the
appellant started asserting rights over
the  subject  land  in  the  year  2010.
Therefore,  before  that,  the  Writ
Petitioner was not aware that the land has
been allotted to the present appellant.
5. Basing  this  statement,  the  High
Court proceeded to examine the matter on
merits  and  allowed  the  Writ  Petition
mainly on the ground that before allotting
the subject land to the appellant,  the

--- Page 129 ---
3
mandate of Rule 95 A of the Rules under
Assam Land and Revenue Regulation, 1886
(for short, ‘the Grazing Rules’), has not
been  followed.  According  to  the  High
Court,  the  allotment  was  made  on
11.09.1970 and the same was approved by
the  State  Government  on  06.10.1970.
However, the de-reservation was made only
on 15.11.1972. Therefore, the allotment
was made when the subject land was still
treated as grazing land by the Government.
6. We have heard Mr. Vijay Hansaria,
learned senior counsel appearing for the
appellant, Mr. Tapan Ranjan Deuri, learned
counsel appearing for respondent no.1 and
Mr.  Debojit  Borkakati,  learned  counsel
appearing for the State at length and
perused the papers.
7. In our view, this appeal deserves
to be allowed on more than one ground,
firstly, Rule 95A of the Grazing Rules
provides for de-reservation of land which
is declared as grazing ground under the
Rules. However, before de-reserving the

--- Page 130 ---
4
land, there has to be a procedure followed
for reserving the  land for allotment of
grazing ground. Secondly,  Rules for the
Allotment of Grazing Grounds in Chapter-II
of the Grazing Rules provides for steps to
be taken and procedure to be followed for
allotment of grazing grounds which, inter
alia provides that for declaring any such
land as grazing land, firstly, there has
to  be  a  survey  and  demarcation  and
thereafter  publication  of  notice  about
Government’s proposal to allot the land as
grazing ground. Thereafter, the concerned
officer, i.e. the Deputy Commissioner, has
to  hear  objections  and  conclude  the
proceedings and issue a declaration under
Rule 91 of the Rules. The said Rule 91 of
the Grazing Rules is reproduced here for
ready reference;
“91.   Declaration  of  grazing
grounds – As soon as the cost of
demarcation has been deposited the
Deputy Commissioner shall cause to
be  published  in  the  manner
prescribed  in  rule  85,  a  final
notice declaring the land to be
allotted as grazing ground.  He
shall also cause the grazing ground

--- Page 131 ---
5
to be entered in the register of
grazing grounds and the boundaries
thereof to be demarcated with such
boundary-marks as may be required.”
8. In the documents placed before this
Court, there is no indication as to at
which  particular  point  in  time,  the
subject  land  was  declared  as  grazing
ground. Even if the State Government later
on de-reserved the land vide its order
dated 15.11.1972, this order itself does
not indicate that the land was reserved at
a particular point in time. It seems that
the de-reservation was made because some
villagers started raising objections to
the allotment of land to the appellant.
9. Be  that  as  it  may,  the  Writ
Petition was filed after 40 years from the
date of the allotment.
10. Learned State counsel would argue,
supporting the appellant, that from the
date of allotment till the date of filing
of the Writ Petition, the appellant was
exercising his rights as possession was
delivered to the appellant on 02.04.1971
(Annexure P-7).

--- Page 132 ---
6
11. Thus,  once  the  appellant  was
settled in possession in the year 1971
itself, a bald statement by the first
respondent/  Writ  Petitioner  that  the
present  appellant  never  asserted  his
rights over the land for 40 years is
unbelievable.
12. For both the above reasons, i.e. in
view of the provisions of Grazing Rules as
also on the ground of delay in filing the
Writ Petition, the High Court committed
error  of  law  by  allowing  the  Writ
Petition.
13. Accordingly,  the  appeal  stands
allowed. The impugned order passed by the
High Court is set aside to hold that the
appellant  shall  continue  to  enjoy  the
subject land on the basis of allotment
made  in  its  favour  vide  communication
dated  11.09.1970  issued  by  the  Deputy
Commissioner, and subsequently approved by
the State Government vide communication
dated 06.10.1970.

--- Page 133 ---
7
14. Pending  application(s),  if  any,
shall stand closed.
......................J.
 (PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA)
.....................J.
       (SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR)
New Delhi
14-07-2026

--- Page 134 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1358/2026
IN
CIVIL APPEAL No.4167/2012
M.T. ENRICA LEXIE & ANR.                        Applicants
                             VERSUS
DORAMMA & ORS.                           Respondents
O R D E R
1. We have heard Mr. K. K. Venugopal, learned senior counsel
for the applicant and Mr. S. V. Raju, learned Additional Solicitor
General assisted by Mr. Annam Venkatesh and Mr. Aljo K. Joseph,
learned counsel appearing for the State of Kerala.
2. This is an application seeking direction for modification of
the order dated 02 nd May, 2012 in Civil Appeal No.4167 of 2012.
The operative portion of the order dated 02 nd May, 2012 reads as
follows:
“26. Having regard to the above, we dispose of the present Appeal
by the following order :-
(1) Subject to the compliances by the appellants as noted below, the
Government  of  Kerala  and  its  authorities  shall  allow  the  first
appellant vessel to commence her voyage :-
 (a)  The  Master  of  the  first  appellant  vessel,  the  Managing
Director  of  the  owner  of  the  first  appellant  vessel  and  the
1

--- Page 136 ---
Managing  Director  of  the  shipping  agent,  namely,  James
Mackintosh & Co. Pvt. Ltd shall furnish their undertakings to the
satisfaction of the Registrar General of the Kerala High Court that
six  crew  members,  namely,  Vitelli  Umberto  (Master),  Noviello
Carlo (Master SN), James Mandley Samson (Chief Officer), Sahil
Gupta  (2nd  Officer),  Fulbaria  (Seaman)  and  Tirumala  Rao
(Ordinary Sea Man), on receipt of summons/notice from any court
or  by  Investigating  Officer  or  lawful  authority  shall  present
themselves within five weeks from the date of the receipt of such
summons/notice and shall produce the first appellant vessel, if
required by any court or the Investigating Officer or any other
lawful authority, within seven weeks from the receipt of such
summons/notice. 
(b) The second appellant shall execute a bond in the sum of
Rupees Three Crores before the Registrar General of the Kerala
High  Court  for  production  of  the  first  appellant  vessel  and
securing the presence of the above six crew members as and
when called upon by any court or the Investigating Officer or any
other lawful authority.
(2) The assurance given by the Republic of Italy that if the presence
of the four Marines, namely, Voglino Renato (Seargeant), Andronico
Massimo (1 st Corporal), Fontano Antonio (3rd Corporal) and Conte
Alessandro (Corporal), is required by any court or lawful authority or
Investigating  Officer,  the  Republic  of  Italy  shall  ensure  their
presence  before  such  court  or  lawful  authority  or  Investigating
Officer is accepted. Such assurance shall, however, not affect the
right of the above four Marines to challenge such summons/notice
issued  by  any  court  or  Investigating  Officer  or  any  other  lawful
authority before a competent court in India.
27.  It  is  clarified  that  the  investigation  into  Crime  No.  2/2012
registered  at  Neendakara  Coastal  Police  Station  shall  not  be  an
impediment for commencement of the voyage by the first appellant
vessel subject to port and customs clearances in accordance with
law and upon furnishing the undertakings and bond as noted above.
28.  The  four  Marines,  namely,  Voglino  Renato  (Seargeant),
Andronico Massimo (1 st Corporal), Fontano Antonio (3rd Corporal)
2

--- Page 137 ---
and Conte Alessandro (Corporal), may sail on the vessel together
with  all  equipments,  arms  and  ammunitions  on  board  the  first
appellant vessel other than those already seized by the Investigating
Officer.”
3. It is not in dispute that the prosecution stands terminated by
reason of quashing of the FIR by this Court vide order dated 15 th
June, 2021. Operative part of the said order reads as follows:-
“7. In view of the above and for the reasons stated above and in
exercise of the powers under Article 142 of the Constitution of India,
we dispose of/close the present proceedings by directing as under:
(a) FIR No.2/2012 of Coastal PS, Neendakara, Kollam, Kerala re-
registered as FIR No. R.C. No. 04/2013/NIA/DLI dated 4 April 2013,
under Sections 302, 307, 427 read with Section 34 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 and Section 3 of the Suppression of Unlawful
Acts Against Safety of Maritime Navigation and Fixed Platforms
on  Continental  Shelf  Act,  2002  registered  by  the  National
Investigation Agency, New Delhi, and all proceedings emanating
therefrom  including  the  proceedings  pending  before  the  Ld.
Special Designated Court, Patiala House Courts, New Delhi are
hereby quashed; 
(b) The Bail-Bonds dated 2 June 2012 executed by Chief Master
Sergeant  Massimiliano  Latorre  and  Sergeant  Major  Salvatore
Girone as also Mr. Vishal Talwar and Mr. Vikas Talwar who stood
as Sureties, in connection with the aforementioned FIR before the
Ld. Special Designated Court, Patiala House Courts, New Delhi
and  the  Ld.  Registrar  General  of  this  Court  are  hereby
discharged; 
(c)  The  Ld.  Registrar  General  of  this  Court  shall  release  the
original  Bank  Guarantees  bearing  Nos.0071IGFIN000618  and
0071IGFIN000418, both dated 11 June 2018 for Rs. Two Crores
each, issued by the UCO Bank, Kollam Branch, extended through
Letters  of  Extension  Nos.  UCO/KOLLAM/BG/02/2020-21  and
UCO/KOLLAM/BG/01/2020-21 dated 28 May 2020, given on behalf
3

--- Page 138 ---
of the two Sureties, Mr. Vikas Talwar and Mr. Vishal Talwar;
(d) All pending matters before this Hon’ble Court including (1) the
Special Leave Petition (C) No. 20370 of 2012, (2) Writ Petition (C)
No. 236 of 2014, (3) Writ Petition (C) No. 919 of 2014 and all
pending I.As in the said proceedings are disposed of with no order
as to costs.
(e) As per the award dated 21.05.2020 and even as agreed by
the learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of the Republic of
Italy, learned Solicitor General appearing on behalf of the Union
of India and the learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of the
State of Kerala, now the Republic of Italy shall resume its criminal
investigation in the events of 15.02.2012 and it is further directed
that the Union of India, Republic of Italy and the State of Kerala
shall cooperate with each other in pursuit of that investigation. 
8. We also further direct that the amount of Rs. Ten Crores now lying
with the Registry of this Court be transferred to the High Court of
Kerala, out of which Rupees Four Crores be paid to the heirs of each
deceased and Rs. Two crores be paid to the owner of the boat – St.
Antony. We request the Hon’ble Chief Justice of the Kerala High
Court  to  nominate  a  Judge  to  pass  appropriate  order  of
disbursement/investment of the amount to be paid to the heirs of
each  deceased  (Rupees  Four  Crores  each)  so  as  to  protect  the
interest  of  the  heirs  and  ensure  that  the  compensation  is  duly
received by the heirs and not diverted/misappropriated. The order of
disbursement/investment be passed after hearing the heirs of each
deceased  and  appropriate  order  be  passed,  protecting  the  best
interest of the heirs of each deceased. The remaining amount of Rs.
Two Crores be paid to the owner of the boat – St. Antony by an
account payee cheque.
4. It  is  in  these  circumstances  that  the  applicant  has
approached this Court with the miscellaneous application seeking
the following relief:
a)  To  modify  the  order  dated  02.05.2012  and  may  direct  the
4

--- Page 139 ---
Registrar General of High Court of Kerala to release/return the Bond
of  Three  Crores,  and  all  undertakings  so  submitted  the
Appellant/Applicant  in  WP(C)  No.6083  of  2012  from  which  Writ
Appeal No.679 of 2012 was filed and later the Civil Appeal No.4167
of 2012 was preferred by the Petitioner before this Hon'ble Court.
5. We  have  found  from  a  letter  dated 27th December  2024
issued by the Registrar General, High Court of Kerala, Ernakulam
addressed  to  the  applicant’s  advocate  that  1)  undertaking
submitted by Captain Umberto Vitelli, 2) undertaking submitted by
Mr. Farokh  H Commissariat, Managing  Director,  3)  undertaking
submitted by Mr. Schiano Lomoriello Pio, Managing Director and 4)
Bond  for  an  amount  of  Rs.3,00,00,000/-  are  lying  in  the  safe
custody of the Registrar General and forms part of the records.
6. It also appears therefrom that a demand draft dated 05 th
May, 2012 for Rs.3,00,00,000/- drawn on Standard Chartered Bank
has been returned to Advocate Bijish B. Tom, counsel for the 2 nd
respondent in Civil Appeal No.4167/2012 on 09 th May, 2012 on
substituting  the  same  with  a  fixed  deposit  receipt  and  that
consequent upon an order dated 12 th July, 2013 in Civil Appeal
No.4167 of 2012, the aforesaid fixed deposit receipt has been
released  to  the  appellant  no.2  viz.  Dolphin  Tanker  SRL
represented by Mr. Jacob John, Manager, James Mackintosh & Co.
Private Ltd.
7. Having noted the aforesaid state of affairs, we dispose of the
miscellaneous application by ordering modification of the order
5

--- Page 140 ---
dated 02nd May, 2012 to the extent that the applicants shall stand
discharged of the undertakings that they had given pursuant to
the said order.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.8147  /2026
ARUN PATI TRIPATHI ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF JHARKHAND ..... RESPONDENT
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.8121  /2026
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellants apprehending their arrest in
the light of FIR No.09/2025 registered by P.S.:
Anti-Corruption  Bureau  for  the  offences
punishable under Section 61(2) read with Sections
318, 336, 340, 316, 45 and 49 of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023  and Sections 7(c), 12 and
13(2)  read  with  Section  13(1)(a)  of  the
1

--- Page 144 ---
Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988  are seeking
for being enlarged on anticipatory bail since
their prayer before the courts-below has been
negatived.
4. The case of the prosecution is Jharkhand
State Beverages Corporation Limited had engaged
two placement agencies for manpower supply at its
retail  liquor  stores  and  these  agencies  had
submitted fake bank guarantees and investigation
revealed  a  deep  rooted  conspiracy  where
appellants alongwith Anwar Dhebar had allegedly
paid Rs.5 Crores as a bribe to Vinay Kumar
Choubey, then Principal Secretary, Excise and
Prohibition Department, Government of Jharkhand
in January, 2022 to secure the advisory role for
Chhattisgarh State Marketing Corporation Limited.
The prosecution specifically attributes the role
to Arun Pati Tripathi to be the chief conspirator
and  accused  of  paying  the  bribe  amount  and
subsequently,  facilitating  the  appointment  of
specific placement agencies in Chhattisgarh and
2

--- Page 145 ---
Jharkhand which resulted in financial loss to the
State. By the impugned order, the prayer of the
appellants has been rejected on the ground that
fake  guarantees  had  been  submitted  by  two
placement agencies and it was found that the
appellants had close links with main accused.
Hence,  on  the  ground  that  the  custodial
interrogation  is  necessary  to  confront  the
appellants with other co-accused, prayer came to
be rejected. As to whether the appellants acted
purely in an advisory capacity with no decision
making authority are all issues which will have
to be thrashed out after evaluating the evidence
that  would  be  tendered  by  the  prosecution.
Undisputedly, appellants were not initially named
in the FIR and based on the co-accused statement,
they have been implicated. In fact, the main
accused Vinay Kumar Choubey is on default bail
and the co-accused Vinay Kumar Singh has also
been granted anticipatory bail and two other co-
accused have been granted interim protection by
3

--- Page 146 ---
this Court. In the light of these facts and also
taking note of the fact that this Court while
issuing notice on the above appeals had directed
the appellants to appear before the Investigating
Officer and the learned counsel appearing for the
respondent  –  State  is  not  disputing  their
appearance before the Investigating Officer as
ordered by this Court, we see no reason as to why
the interim order granted should not be made
absolute.  Hence,  we  allow  these  appeals  and
direct  the  appellants  to  be  released  on
anticipatory  bail  by  the  jurisdictional
Investigating Officer subject to such terms and
conditions as he deems fit to impose and we make
it clear that both the appellants shall appear
before the Investigating Officer as and when
called upon or informed (even over phone) and
shall cooperate with the investigation fully. In
the event of the appellants failing to appear or
not  cooperating  with  the  investigation,  the
respondent – State would be at liberty to move
4

--- Page 147 ---
the jurisdictional trial court for cancellation
of their bail by filing appropriate application
and in the event of such an application being
filed, the jurisdictional Court shall consider
the same on its own merits and in accordance with
law, necessarily after giving opportunity to the
appellants herein. Accordingly, the appeals stand
disposed of. 
5. All pending applications stand disposed of.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.               OF 2026
(@ SLP (Crl.) No.9961/2023)
RAVINDRA KUMAR SINGH                      ...APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND & ORS.                     ... RESPONDENT(S)
   
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.                OF 2026
(@ SLP (Crl.) No.10037/2023)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. These appeals are directed against common order dated 28.06.2023 passed by the
High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi (High Court) in W.P. (Cr) No. 71 of 2019 and
W.P. (Cr) No. 332 of 2017. By the said order, the High Court has dismissed the
writ petitions under Article 226, seeking to quash two criminal proceedings for
offences under Sections 467, 468, 471, 420, 379, 411 and 414/34 arising out of
P.S. Case No. 11 of 2015 pending before Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) and FIR
for offences under Sections 188, 379, 411, 420, 467, 468, 471, 355 r/w Section 34
of the Indian Penal Code arising out of P.S. Case No. 35 of 2017. 
1

--- Page 151 ---
3. The appellant in Criminal Appeal @ S.L.P. (Crl) No. 9961 of 2023, is  the Chief
Executive Officer of Corporate Ispat Alloys Limited whereas in Criminal Appeal
@ S.L.P. (Crl) No.10037 of 2023 appellant No. 1 is the Chief Executive Officer
and appellant No. 2 is the Managing Director of Corporate Ispat Alloys Limited.
On 21.01.2015, iron rods were being carried in six trucks illegally which were
intercepted. Thereupon first information report was registered against the appellant
in Criminal Appeal @  S.L.P. (Crl) No. 9961 of 2023  for offences under Sections
467, 468, 471, 420, 379, 411 and Section 414/34  on the ground that it was the
property of  M/s Abhijit Project Limited (now known as “M/s Path Breaking
Project Limited”) which is under liquidation and the same is the stolen property.
Subsequently,  on  12.08.2017,  scrap  and  raw  material  of  M/s  Abhijit  Project
Limited, was being transported in three trucks which were intercepted. Thereupon,
a first information report was filed against appellants in Criminal Appeal @ S.L.P.
(Crl) No.10037 of 2023 under Sections 188, 379 and 411, 420, 467, 468 and 471,
Section 355 and Section 34 of the Indian Penal Code.
4. The appellant in Criminal Appeal @ S.L.P. (Crl) No. 9961 of 2023 filed a petition
on 22.01.2017 under Section 482 of the Cr.P.C. before the High Court, which was
withdrawn on 25.01.2019 with the liberty to file a writ petition under Article 226.
The appellant in Criminal Appeal @  S.L.P. (Crl) No. 9961 of 2023 thereupon filed
a writ petition seeking to quash the proceedings pending before the CJM. The
appellants in Criminal Appeal @ S.L.P. (Crl) No. 10037 of 2023, filed a writ
2

--- Page 152 ---
petition  under  Article  226  before  the  High  Court  seeking  quashment  of  first
information report (P.S. Case No. 35 of 2017). The High Court by a common order
dated 28.06.2023  inter alia  held that the defence taken by appellants regarding
valid sale, invoices, and tax payments involves disputed questions of facts which
cannot be adjudicated in a writ petition. The High Court concluded that the cases
do  not fall within  the parameters warranting  quashing  of  proceedings and  the
appellants were granted liberty to raise all the defences during trial. Accordingly,
the writ petitions were dismissed. 
5. This Court by order dated 22.08.2023 entertained the Special Leave Petitions and
thereafter by an order dated 21.10.2024 directed that no coercive steps shall be
taken against the appellants. 
6. Mr. Navniti Prasad Singh, learned senior counsel for the appellants submitted that
transactions in question were bona fide commercial sale of iron rods and scrap
material undertaken to discharge liabilities towards the contractors and workers
which were supported by valid invoices, tax payments and prior intimation to the
authorities. Learned senior counsel has also invited the attention of this Court to
I.A.  No.  190252  of  2026,  an  application  for  permission  to  file  additional
documents and has submitted that during the pendency of Special Leave Petitions
criminal case arising out of P.S. No. 11 of 2015, which had culminated into a
separate  split-up  trial  registered  as  G.R.  Case  (N)  No.  76  of  2015,  has  been
decided  by  the  CJM,  Seraikella  Kharsawan  by  judgment  dated  12.03.2026,
whereby all the accused persons facing trial therein have been acquitted of all the
3

--- Page 153 ---
charges. Our attention has also been invited to judgment dated 12.03.2026 passed
by  the  CJM.  It  is,  therefore,  contended  that  impugned  common  order  dated
28.06.2023 be quashed and set aside.
7.  On the other hand, Mr. Vishnu Sharma, learned standing counsel for the State of
Jharkhand, submitted that FIR discloses commission of cognizable offence and the
assets of the company which were subject to restriction in liquidation proceedings
were being sold and removed without proper authorization. It is contended that no
case for quashing the criminal proceeding and the FIR is made out. However,
learned  standing  counsel  for  the  State  did  not  dispute  that  judgment  dated
12.03.2026 has been passed by the CJM arising out of same P.S. Case No. 11 of
2015 by which the accused therein have been acquitted of the charges against
them. 
8.  We have considered the rival submissions made. In view of the subsequent event,
namely judgment dated 12.03.2026 passed by CJM arising out of same P.S. Case
No. 11 of 2015 wherein CJM after full-dress trial has acquitted the accused therein,
we set aside the common order dated 28.06.2023 passed by the High Court in W.P.
(Cr) No. 332 of 2017 and W.P. (Cr) No. 71 of 2019 and restore the writ petitions.
We  request  the High Court to decide the writ petitions afresh, by taking into
account the judgment dated 12.03.2026 passed by CJM in G.R. No. 76 of 2015
arising out of P.S. Case No. 11 of 2015 within a period of three months. The
interim order granted by this Court dated 21.10.2024 shall continue till decision of
the writ petitions. It is made clear that this Court has not expressed any opinion on
4

--- Page 154 ---
the merits of the case. 
9. With the aforesaid directions, the Appeals are disposed of. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.            
  
………………………………………………J.
            [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
   
………………………………………………J.
                    [ALOK ARADHE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026
5

--- Page 155 ---

       UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. When this matter was last heard on 24th April, 2026, it was
brought to our notice and in fact we recorded so in our Order that
a Committee of three Hon’ble Judges has been constituted by the
Hon’ble Chief Justice of the High Court to look into the issues in
question. We also clarified that it would be open for the Committee
to have an interaction with the Chief Secretary and see to it that
the issues are resolved at the earliest. 
2. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing, Mr.
P.S. Patwalia, the learned Senior counsel appearing for the High
Court and Mr. Parth Awasti, the learned counsel appearing for the
State, brought to our notice few developments that have taken place
during the interregnum period. 
3. It appears that the Committee of the three Hon’ble Judges
resolved in its meeting dated 19-5-2026 to recommend that the
Registrar General should have a meeting with the Chief Secretary of
the Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir for discussing the
modalities for giving effect to the recommendations of the Shetty
Pay Commission from 1-4-2003 in terms of Para 3 of the Order passed
by this Court. 
4. The Committee further resolved that by any chance if the
issues are not sorted out then the Registrar General shall prepare
an agenda and fix a meeting of the Committee of the three Hon’ble
Judges with the Chief Secretary of the Union Territory of Jammu and
Kashmir. 
5. It appears that a letter was addressed on 9-6-2026 by the High
Court to the Financial Commissioner (Additional Chief Secretary),
stating as under:-
“To accord retrospective effect to the promotion of eligible
officials/officers of the J&K District Judiciary in tune with the
Judgement of the Hon'ble Supreme Court for benefits in terms of the
Shetty Pay Commission, an extensive. exercise is required to be
undertaken for the identification of eligibis officers/officials for
promotion  against  the  posts  created  pursuant  to  Shetty  Pay
Commission, determination of their respective dates of entitlement,
fixation of pay thereto.

3
In the above backdrop, I am under instructions to request your
good  self  to  kindly  depute  the  following  number  of
officers/officials from the Finance Department for carrying out the
above said assignments:
1. Accounts Officers: 2
2 Assistant Accounts Officers: 2
3.Accountants: 4
4. Accounts Assistants: 4
This is in reference to the meeting held under the Chairmanship
of the Hon'ble Chief Secretary of the UT of J&K held on 03-06-2026
in pursuance of the Hon'ble Supreme Court order dated 24-04-2026.”

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.           OF 2026)
(@SLP(CRL.) D. NO.34363 OF 2026)
RAJU PRASAD ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Leave granted.
4. An FIR No.85/2025 came to be registered against the
appellant  and  others  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections 318(4), 338, 326(3) and 61(2)(a) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS) alleging that the appellant was an
attesting witness to a forged Power of Attorney and also the
subsequent sales transactions concerning agriculture land
based on the said forged Power of Attorney. The courts below
have rejected the prayer of the appellant.
1                                                                              SLP(CRL.) D. NO.34363 OF 2026

--- Page 170 ---
5. As noted from the prosecution theory it was alleged that
neither the complainant nor the co-sharers of the immovable
property had ever executed a Power of Attorney or transferred
the land. The signatures found on the Power of Attorney as
well as the Sale Deeds, are all forged.
6. It is the case of the prosecution that accused persons
conspired  to  fabricate  documents  and  transfer  the  land
through Bhavani Shankar Sanwarmal and Dharam Pratap Singh.
The appellant and another co-accused Sanwarmal are alleged to
have signed as a witness to the said documents having
knowledge that they were forged documents.
7. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State has
vehemently opposed the bail contending inter alia that the
trial is progressing and the appellant and other accused
having formed a syndicate and are indulging in such acts of
cheating people and depriving them of their valuable property
and if enlarged on bail they are likely to indulge in similar
acts. He would also draw the attention of the Court to the
order  of  the  trial  court  and  also  the  impugned  order
whereunder  it  has  been  noticed  that  there  are  alleged
criminal antecedents against the appellant. Refuting the
same, the learned counsel appearing for the appellant has
2                                                                              SLP(CRL.) D. NO.34363 OF 2026

--- Page 171 ---
contended that out of four cases, in one case appellant has
been granted the benefit of Probation of Offenders Act (PO
Act). He would also submit that in none of those cases the re
are any allegation of cheating and as such he seeks for being
enlarged on bail.
8. Having heard the learned counsels appearing for the
parties and having taken note of the fact that the appellant
is in incarceration for more than 11 months and the trail is
progressing, we see no reason as to why he should be kept in
the continued confinement. To allay the apprehension of the
prosecution, it would suffice to impose strict conditions for
being enlarged on bail.
9. Accordingly, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set
aside. Appellant is ordered to be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as the jurisdictional trial court may
deem fit to impose and also on the condition that appellant
would appear before the jurisdictional trial court on all
dates of hearing except when exempted for any specific
reason.
3                                                                              SLP(CRL.) D. NO.34363 OF 2026

--- Page 172 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
New Delhi;
July 13, 2026.

Leave granted.
The  appellants  in  Criminal  Appeal  @  SLP
(Crl.)  No.3498/2026  apprehend  their  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.247/2025 dated 13.08.2025,
registered  at  Police  Station  Mico  Layout,
1

--- Page 176 ---
2
District-Bengaluru City, Circle/Sub-Division-Mico
Layout, for the offences punishable under Sections
3(5), 80(2), 85, 108, 238 and 49 of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 along with Sections 3 and 4 of
the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961.
The appellant in Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.)
No.3662/2026 seeks regular bail in connection with
the same. 
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
We have been informed that the charge sheet
has been filed.
Insofar as the appellants in Criminal Appeal
@ SLP (Crl.) No.3498/2026 are concerned, they are
the in-laws of the deceased, while the appellant
in Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.3662/2026 is
the husband of the deceased and has undergone
around 11 months of incarceration.
The first appellant in Criminal Appeal @ SLP
2

--- Page 177 ---
3
(Crl.) No.3498/2026, being the father-in-law of
the deceased is a Government servant, while the
second  appellant  being  the  mother-in-law,  is
suffering  from  cardiac  ailments.   The  third
appellant is the sister-in-law of the deceased,
who lives far away.
Taking  note  of  the  aforesaid  facts,
particularly the fact that the charge sheet has
been filed, we are inclined to set aside the
impugned order and grant anticipatory bail to the
appellants  in  Criminal  Appeal  @  SLP  (Crl.)
No.3498/2026 and regular bail to the appellant in
Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.3662/2026.
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside.
The appellants in Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.)
NO.3498/2026 are granted anticipatory bail, and
the appellant in Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.)
No.3662/2026 is granted regular bail, on terms and
3

--- Page 178 ---
4
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court.  
The appeals are allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
