IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.3215 OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.5804/2026]
SAGAR SINHA                 APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.    RESPONDENTS
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The  High  Court  of  Punjab  &  Haryana  at
Chandigarh,  by  the  impugned  judgment  and  order
dated  10 th February,  2026  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest. 
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No.109 of
2025  dated  12th August,  2025  registered  at  Police
Station  Bhogpur,  District  Jalandhar  Rural  under
Sections  316(2)  &  318(4)  of  the  Bharatiya  Nyaya
Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.5804/2026 1

--- Page 182 ---
Sanhita (BNS), 2023 and Section 13 of the Punjab
T ravel Professionals (Regulation) Act, 2014.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for
the  appellant  as  well  as  respondent  no.1-State  of
Punjab.
5. Though  belatedly,  a ppellant  has  joined
investigation in terms of the earlier order of this
Court.
6. It  is  also  undisputed  that  the  appellant  has
returned  Rs.10,00,000/-  (Rupees  ten  lakh  only)  of
Rs.13,00,000/- (Rupees thirteen lakh only) he owed to
the complainant.
7.  Having regard to such facts and circumstances,
we are of the considered opinion that  the appellant’s
detention for custodial interrogation is not necessary
and that he may be admitted to an order for grant of
bail in anticipation of arrest.
8. We,  accordingly,  set  aside  the  judgment  and
Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.5804/2026 2

--- Page 183 ---
order impugned in this appeal.
9. It is directed that in the event of the appellant
being arrested, he shall be released on bail on terms
and conditions to be imposed by the trial court.
10. Since  the investigation is yet to conclude, we
direct that if the investigating officer calls upon the
appellant  to  continue  to  join  the  investigation,  he
shall  do  so  by  attending  the  police  station  failing
which liberty is granted to bring any such omission to
the notice of the trial court and seek cancellation of
bail.
11. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,
directly or indirectly, by making inducement, threat
or promise, dissuade any person acquainted with the
facts of the case from disclosing such facts to any
police officer or to the court.
12. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in  this
order and grant of bail to the appellant in anticipation
Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.5804/2026 3

--- Page 184 ---
of arrest will not be treated as findings on the merits
of the case.
13. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed  on  the
aforesaid terms.
14. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.

O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The  High  Court  of  Punjab  &  Haryana  at
Chandigarh,  by  the  impugned  judgment  and  order
dated 10 th March, 2026 has rejected the appellant’s
prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest. 
Criminal Appeal @ SLP(Crl.) No. 6586/2026 
1

--- Page 188 ---
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No.0122
of 2025 dated 29 th August, 2025 registered at Police
Station Sangat, District Bathinda, under Sections 74,
126(2),  115(2),  117(2)  and  3(5)  of  the  Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for
the parties.
5. We have also perused the counter affidavit filed
by the respondent-State of Punjab. It is stated therein
that the appellant has joined the investigation, the
injuries suffered by the victim are simple, and that
custodial  interrogation  of  the  appellant  is  not
necessary.
6. Having regard thereto, we are of the considered
opinion  that there  is  no  impediment  to  admit  the
appellant to an order for grant of bail in anticipation
of arrest.
Criminal Appeal @ SLP(Crl.) No. 6586/2026 
2

--- Page 189 ---

order impugned in this appeal.

directly or indirectly, by making inducement, threat
or promise, dissuade any person acquainted with the
facts of the case from disclosing such facts to any
police officer or to the court.
10. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in  this
order and grant of bail to the appellant in anticipation
of arrest will not be treated as findings on the merits

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No. 8968 OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) No.18587/2023
VIKASH SINGH ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
DIVISIONAL MANAGER THE ORIENTAL 
INSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED & ORS.
..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. None appears for the appellant on call, though Mr. Rajesh
Kumar  Gupta,  learned  counsel  is  present  on  behalf  of  the
respondent-Oriental Insurance Company Limited.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by the impugned judgment and order
dated  28.03.2023  passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh at
Jabalpur (briefly the 'High Court' hereinafter) allowing Misc.
Appeal No. 5200 of 2022 filed by the respondent.
4. Respondent had preferred the aforesaid appeal, being aggrieved
by the award of compensation totaling 34,08,116.00 (rupees thirty₹
four lakh eight thousand one hundred and sixteen only), passed by
the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal in favour of the appellant.
Without entering into the rival contentions, what we notice is that
on the date when the appeal was taken up for hearing, there was a
strike call given by the Bar Council of Madhya Pradesh because of
which lawyers were not present in the court. While certainly the
High Court was justified in adversely commenting upon such strike,
1

--- Page 200 ---
the High Court went ahead and disposed of the appeal in the absence
of lawyers.
5. We are of the view that it would meet the ends of justice if
the matter is remanded back to the High Court for a fresh
consideration on merit after hearing learned counsel for both the
sides. In this regard, we may draw the attention of Order XLI Rule
17 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which reads as follows:
"(1)  Where on the day fixed, or on any
other  day  which  the  hearing  may  be
adjourned, the appellant does not appear
when the appeal is called on for hearing,
the Court may make an order that the appeal
be dismissed.
 [Explanation.—Nothing in this sub-rule 
shall be construed as empowering the Court 
to dismiss the appeal on the merits.]
(2)  Hearing appeal ex parte – Where the
appellant appears and the respondent does
not appear the appeal shall be heard ex
parte."
6. In the present case, the High Court went ahead and allowed the
appeal of the respondent even when there is non appearance.
7. That being the position and without expressing any opinion on
merit, we set aside the impugned judgment and order of the High
Court  dated  28.03.2023  with  the  direction  that  Misc.  Appeal
No.5200/2022  be  heard  and  decided  afresh  after  giving  due
opportunity of hearing to learned counsel for both the sides.
8. Civil Appeal is accordingly disposed of.
2

--- Page 201 ---
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 14, 2026.
IZ
3

--- Page 202 ---

     O R D E R
1. In our order dated 09.09.2024, it
is observed that the matter is listed for
not successfully effecting the service of
notice on the sole appellant for making
alternative arrangement of counsel.

appellant to press the appeal.

which, leave was granted in the year 2009.

18  years  and  the  appellant  is  not
represented.

no other option but to dismiss the appeal
for want of prosecution.

2
5. Accordingly,  the  appeal  is

liberty in favour of the appellant to
revive the matter if the cause of action
still survives.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Civil Appeal  No(s).  7227/2009
1. The appeal is dismissed for want of
prosecution in terms of the signed order.
2. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.
ORDERS ON SLP(C) No. 25481/2010
1. The  order  assailed  in  this
Special  Leave  Petition  is  the  order
passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay  in  Appeal  No.  640  of  2007
dismissing the appeal preferred by the
fourth  respondent,  Hatimi  Steels.  The
petitioner being one of the respondents
in the appeal before the High Court can
never be said to be aggrieved by an order
by which the other parties’ appeal was
dismissed.
2. At this stage, learned counsel
appearing for the petitioner submits that
in fact, the petitioner had suffered an
adverse order in Appeal No.703 of 2007
and by inadvertence, the order passed in
Appeal No. 640 of 2007 has been annexed

--- Page 213 ---
5
and impugned in the appeal.
3. We have seen the impugned order
as also the prayer(s) made in the Special
Leave Petition. In the prayer clause, the
petitioner prays for setting aside the
order dated 06 th July, 2010 passed by the
High Court in Appeal No. 640 of 2007.
4. Therefore, the submission made by
the learned counsel for the petitioner
does not appear to be well founded.
5. In view of the above, the Special
Leave  Petition  is  dismissed.  If  the
petitioner  is  aggrieved  by  the  order
passed by the High Court in Appeal No.
703 of 2007, it will remain open to the
petitioner to take recourse to the remedy
as is available in law.
6. Pending application(s), if any,
shall stand disposed of.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH) 
 (signed order in Civil Appeal No. 7227/2009 is placed on the file)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL NO. ______ OF 2026
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (C) NO. ________ OF 2026
[DIARY NO. 71235 OF 2025]
THE INDIAN HUME PIPE COMPANY LIMITED          Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
BHILAI MUNICIPAL CORPORATION & ANR.           Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Delay condoned.  
2. Leave granted.
3. This appeal has been filed against the impugned
Judgment dated 19.06.2025, by which the High Court
has  dismissed  the  writ  petition  filed  by  the
appellant under Article 226 of the Constitution of
India on the ground that the same involves disputed
questions of fact, which cannot be adjudicated in a
writ petition.
4. Brief facts leading to filing of this appeal, in a
nutshell, are that the Bhilai Municipal Corporation
(in short, “the Corporation”) issued two work orders
on  29.11.2016  and  22.07.2017  in  favour  of  the
appellant  for  the  design,  laying,  jointing,
interconnection, testing and commissioning of clear
water rising main under the Bhilai Augmentation of
Water Supply Scheme Part II and for providing the
design and construction of 10 nos. of PHSR in the

--- Page 215 ---
2
Bhilai Municipal areas.  
5. According  to  the  appellant,  it  successfully
completed the works awarded to it on 30.11.2020 and
04.08.2021.
6. The  Corporation,  on  20.02.2022  and  20.09.2022
approved the release of payments in favour of the
appellant towards GST Reimbursement, RA Bill-17 and
18,  retention  money  and  hydrotesting  charges.
However,  the  Corporation  failed  to  release  the
aforesaid amount.
7. The appellant, thereafter, submitted representations
on various dates between 14.03.2024 to 01.01.2025
seeking payment of final bills and other dues.
However,  the  representations  submitted  by  the
appellant failed to evoke any response.  Thereupon,
the  appellant  filed  a  writ  petition  seeking  a
direction to the respondents to make the payment of
the admitted dues, due to it under the contracts
awarded to it.
8. The  High  Court,  by  the  impugned  order  dated
19.06.2025,  inter  alia,  held  that  the  disputed
questions of fact arise for determination which
cannot be adjudicated in a writ petition.  The writ
petition  was,  accordingly,  disposed  of  with  a
liberty to the appellant to take recourse of such

--- Page 216 ---
3
other remedy as may be available to it in law.
9. In the aforesaid factual background, this appeal has
been filed before this Court.
10. We have heard learned senior counsel appearing for
the  parties  at  length.   By  an  order  dated
05.05.2026, we had directed the Corporation to file
a short affidavit indicating the admitted amounts
which may be payable to the appellant.
11. In pursuance of the aforesaid order, two affidavits
dated 04.05.2026 and 18.05.2026 have been filed
before us.  We are not inclined to go into the
details of the averments made in the affidavits, as
in our opinion, in the facts and circumstances of
the case, the disputes between the parties can be
resolved through Arbitration.
12. We, therefore, appoint Mr. Jaideep Gupta, learned
senior counsel, as an Arbitrator to adjudicate the
disputes between the parties. The appellant is at
liberty to file written claim statement and other
documents, including the affidavits filed before
this Court, before the learned Arbitrator.  Equally,
the respondents will also be entitled to file their
suitable response to the claim.  The remuneration
shall be determined by the learned Arbitrator in
consultation  with  the  parties.  The  learned

--- Page 217 ---
4
Arbitrator is requested to conclude the proceedings
within six weeks from today.  
13. In view of above, the civil appeal stands disposed
of.
14. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, is/are
disposed of.  
   ……………………………………………………………………….J.
   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
   …………………………………………………………………...J.
           [ALOK ARADHE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.15512 OF 2025)
VIKAS KUMAR ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF UP & ORS.  ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant who has been arraigned as an accused in FIR
No.177/2025  registered  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections 333, 65(2), 351(3) of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
(BNS) and Sections 3/4 of the Protection of Children from
Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (POCSO) sought for being enlarged on
anticipatory bail, which has been refused by the Courts below,
and as such, appellant is before this Court.
4. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the parties
and on perusal of the material on record, we notice that prior
1                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.15512 OF 2025

--- Page 225 ---
to the registration of the FIR in question, the appellant had
already filed three FIRs against the complainant and her
family members, out of which two have been closed by police
after investigation and, in respect of one FIR, chargesheet is
said to have been filed as stated by the learned counsel
appearing for the State. He would also hasten to add that in
the present FIR, which was initially registered under Section
75(2) of BNS, investigation has been concluded and it has been
found that there is no such rape which has taken place and as
such chargesheet has not been filed for the said offence.
Mr. Shashank Singh, learned AOR appearing for the complainant,
has vehemently opposed the prayer for grant of anticipatory
bail.
5. In view of the same and also taking into consideration that
there is dispute between the parties, we are of the considered
view that appellant would be entitled to the relief of
anticipatory bail.
6. Accordingly, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set
aside. Appellant is ordered to be released on anticipatory
bail on such terms and conditions as the jurisdictional
Investigating Officer (IO) may deem fit to impose and also on
the  condition  that  appellant  would  appear  before  the
2                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.15512 OF 2025

--- Page 226 ---
jurisdictional court on all dates of hearing except when
exempted for any specific reason and cooperating with the
investigation, if any.
7. It is needless to state that the observations made under
the impugned order as well as under this order are confined
only for considering the prayer for grant of anticipatory
bail.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
New Delhi;
July 13, 2026

Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with

City Civil & Sessions Judge, Bengaluru for the
offences punishable under Sections 15, 16, 18 and
20 of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act,

the Indian Penal Code, 1860, Section 4 of the

and Section 2 of the Karnataka Prevention of
Destruction and Loss of Property Act, 1981.

for the appellant and learned ASG appearing for
the respondent.

co-accused persons have been granted bail by this
Court. In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant bail to the appellant.

and the appellant is granted bail on terms and
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 10246 of 2025)
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 18116 of 2025)
AJAY SINGH APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. In  the present  Criminal A ppeals,  this Court  is
concerned only with quantum of sentence imposed upon the
appellant, as notice was issued to this extent only. The
appellant stands convicted for committing offences under
Sections 392 and 354 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 in both the
appeals  and  has  been  awarded  sentence  of  rigorous
imprisonment of five years and rigorous imprisonment of two
years respectively.
3. As  a  matter  of  fact,  the  appellant  was  first
arrested for committing a similar offence which was tried
in Sessions Case No.357 of 2017. In this offence, the
appellant was arrested on 08.01.2017, whereas, in the crime
1

--- Page 245 ---
relating to the present Criminal Appeals, the appellant was
arrested on 13 .01.2017. In the first crime relating to
Sessions Case No.357 of 2017, the appellant has been
awarded sentence of rigorous imprisonment for 10 years.
However,  presently  we  are  not  concerned  with  this
conviction, as these two Criminal Appeals are arising out
of his conviction in Sessions Trial Nos.203 of 2017 and 204
of  2017  rendered  by  First  Additional  Sessions  Judge,
Ujjain.
4. It  is submitted  that as  on date  appellant has
undergone three years and four months in Sessions Trial No.
203 of 2017 and for  one year, five month and twenty seven
days in Sessions Trial No.204 of 2017. The difference in
period undergone in both the matters is for the reason that
the sentences have been awarded in two separate sessions
trials and the sentence after conviction in the second
sessions trial i.e. ST 204 of 2017 has not yet commenced.
5. Be that as it may, we notice that the appellant is
in jail since 08/09.01.2017 i.e. for nearly nine and a half
years.
6. The appellant is presently aged about 60 years.
There is no adverse report by the jail authorities showing
that his conduct in jail was not good.
7. In the above view of the matter, we are of the
considered view that the sentence awarded to the appellant
in Sessions Trial Nos.203 of 2017 and 204 of 2017 rendered
by First Additional Sessions Judge, Ujjain deserves to be
2

--- Page 246 ---
reduced  to  the  period  already  undergone,  we  direct
accordingly by allowing the appeal(s) in part.
8. Accordingly, the appellant be released from jail, if
not required in any other case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  
 ………………………………………………………J.
             [PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA]
   
    ………………………………………………………J.
           [SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR]
NEW DELHI;
July 15, 2026
3

--- Page 247 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.  OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.6897/2026
BABULAL BHARMALJI BISHNOI ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and learned
counsel for the State of Gujarat.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by Order dated 30.06.2025, passed by
the High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad (briefly the ‘High Court’
hereinafter)  rejecting  the  prayer  for  regular  bail  of  the
appellant.
4. It  is  stated  that  appellant  is  an  accused  in  CR  No.
11208002240591 of 2024, registered at Aji Dam Police Station,
Rajkot, under Sections 8(C), 20(b)(ii)(C) and 29 of the Narcotic
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985.
5. On 13.04.2026, this Court had issued notice. Thereafter, State
has filed counter affidavit.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that appellant is in
custody since 28.06.2024, for more than two years now. Out of the
1

--- Page 250 ---
29 cited witnesses, only 4 have been examined. The main contention
of the appellant that the contraband allegedly seized includes
various parts of plants and, therefore, it is doubtful as to
whether the contraband (Ganja) recovered from the premises in which
the appellant was present at the time of seizure, exceeds the
commercial quantity or not. In such circumstances, appellant may be
enlarged on bail.
7. Learned State counsel on the other hand submits that though 29
witnesses have been cited, prosecution will limit the witnesses to
7. 4 have already been examined. Thus, only 3 witnesses remain to
be examined. The next date of the case is 21.07.2026. In so far as
discrepancy in the quantum of the contraband is concerned, she
submits that this is a matter of trial.
8. In response, learned counsel for the appellant submits that it
is for the first time learned counsel for the State is making a
submission that prosecution will examine only 7 witnesses though 29
have been cited. No application in this regard have been moved
before the Trial Court.
9.   After hearing learned counsel for the parties and on due
consideration, we are of the view that appellant may be enlarged on
bail subject to such terms and conditions as may be deemed
appropriate by the Trial Court including deposit of passport, if
any, by the appellant before the Trial Court. In this regard,
appellant shall be produced before the Special Court within a
maximum period of 7 days from today whereafter the appellant shall
be enlarged on bail.
10. In the result, impugned order dated 30.06.2025 passed by the
2

--- Page 251 ---
High Court is set aside.
11. Appeal is accordingly granted.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 16, 2026.
IZ
3

--- Page 252 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No. 8967 OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) No.4745/2024
STATE OF U.P. THROUGH PRINCIPAL 
SECRETARY & ORS.
..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
M/S MANALI VINTRADE PRIVATE LIMITED ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We  have  heard  Ms.  Aishwarya  Bhati,  learned  Additional
Solicitor General of India, for the appellant-State of Uttar
Pradesh and Mr. G aurav Agarwal, learned senior counsel for the
respondent.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by the direction of the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad (briefly the ‘High Court’ hereinafter)
directing refund of the amount of royalty to the respondent for the
period for which he could not carry out mining.
4. This Court by order dated 20.02.2024, had issued notice and
directed stay of paragraph 12 of the impugned order. Paragraph 12
of the impugned order dated 10.10.2023 reads as under:
“(12)  Accordingly,  the  petitioner  be
refunded  the  amount  of  royalty  for  the
period he did not work within a period of
one month. Since the petitioner did not
mine for any fault of his, we also direct
that interest at the rate of 6% per annum
be given to the petitioner on the amount
which shall be paid to him.”
1

--- Page 254 ---
5. After hearing learned counsel for the parties and considering
the materials on record, we are not inclined to interfere with the
order passed by the High Court having regard to the peculiar facts
of the present case.
6.  Consequently, the appeal is dismissed.
7. Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed of.
......................J.

ITEM NO.24               COURT NO.6               SECTION X
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                      RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Writ Petition(s)(Criminal) No(s).221/2026
BABY RITIKA & ORS.                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 170227/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Rohit Kumar Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Puneet Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Silki Trehan, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioners claiming to be home-buyers have invoked the
writ  jurisdiction  of  this  Court  under  Article  32  of  the
Constitution and have prayed for the following reliefs:-
“(a) Issue an appropriate writ, order or direction,
including in the nature of mandamus, directing the
Respondent authorities, particularly the Central Bureau
of  Investigation,  Directorate  of  Enforcement  and
Economic Offences Unit, to conduct a fair, impartial,
comprehensive and court-monitored investigation into the
large-scale real estate fraud perpetrated by M/s Agrani
Homes Private Limited and its group entities, including
all its directors, shareholders, associated persons,
1

--- Page 262 ---
W.P. (Crl.) No.221/2026
beneficiaries and connected corporate entities, so as to
unearth the entire conspiracy and the full chain of
transactions;
(b)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing the Respondents to identify, trace, disclose
and place on record all movable and immovable assets,
bank  accounts,  financial  instruments  and  properties
acquired, directly or indirectly, out of the monies
collected from home buyers, including assets held in the
names  of  relatives,  associates,  shell  entities,
benamidars or proxy holders;
(c)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing attachment, freezing and protection of all
such assets and properties constituting "proceeds of
crime", in accordance with law, including under the
provisions of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act,
2002 and other applicable statutes, so as to prevent
alienation, dissipation or encumbrance thereof during
the pendency of proceedings;
(d)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing the Respondents to take immediate steps for
recovery and restitution of monies unlawfully collected
and  siphoned  off  from  the  Petitioners  and  other
similarly situated home buyers, including by creation of
a transparent and supervised mechanism for distribution
of recovered amounts under the aegis of this Hon'ble
Court;
(e)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing the Respondents to ensure strict enforcement
and execution of orders passed by statutory authorities,
including the Real Estate Regulatory Authority (RERA)
and the Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (REAT), so that
the reliefs granted therein are not rendered illusory or
ineffective;
(f)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing  the  Respondents  to  expand  the  scope  of
investigation to include all directors, officers, family
members, associated entities and beneficiaries of the
Agrani Homes Group, and to take appropriate coercive
2

--- Page 263 ---
W.P. (Crl.) No.221/2026
measures,  including  custodial  interrogation  wherever
necessary, in accordance with law;
(g)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing the Respondents to secure the presence and
cooperation  of  the  accused  persons,  including  by
directing surrender of passports, imposition of travel
restrictions, and such other measures as may be deemed
fit  to  ensure  effective  investigation  and  prevent
evasion of the process of law;
(h)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
appointing or constituting a Court-monitored Special
Investigation  Team  (SIT)  or  any  other  appropriate
mechanism under the supervision of this Hon'ble Court to
ensure  coordinated,  time-bound  and  effective
investigation into all aspects of the fraud;
(i)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
directing the Respondents to submit periodic status
reports before this Hon'ble Court with respect to the
progress of investigation, identification of assets,
attachment proceedings and steps taken for restitution
to the victims;
(j)  Issue  an  appropriate  writ,  order  or  direction
declaring that the failure of the Respondents to conduct
a fair and comprehensive investigation and to secure
restitution  of  monies  amounts  to  violation  of  the
Petitioners' fundamental rights under Articles 14, 21
and 300A of the Constitution of India;
(k) Pass such further or other writ, order or direction
as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the
interest of justice, equity, and fair play, including
but not limited to the award of costs of the present
proceedings in favour of the Petitioner.”
2. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the petitioners
and having looked into the reliefs prayed for, we are of the view
that the very same reliefs could have been prayed for by the
3

--- Page 264 ---
W.P. (Crl.) No.221/2026
petitioners by preferring a writ petition before the territorial
High Court under Article 226 of the Constitution.
3. We permit the petitioners to withdraw this petition, with
liberty to file writ petition before the territorial High Court
seeking for the very same reliefs.
4. If any such writ petition is preferred, the High Court shall
look into the reliefs prayed for on its own merits, in accordance
with law.
5. With the aforesaid, this petition stands disposed of.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 265 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No. 3146 OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.10991/2026
ANILKUMAR ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard Ms. Sheena Taqui, learned counsel for the
appellant and Mr. Rajan Kumar Chourasia, learned counsel for the
respondent-State of Rajasthan.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by the order dated 05.05.2026 passed by
the High Court of Judicature for Rajasthan Bench at Jaipur (briefly
the ‘High Court’ hereinafter) rejecting his bail application.
4. Appellant is an accused in FIR No. 57/2026 registered at
Police Station CPS, Jaipur, District- Anti Corruption Bureau,
Jaipur, for the offence under Sections 7 and 12 of the Prevention
of Corruption Act, 1988 and Section 61(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023.
5. On 08.06.2026, this Court had issued notice pursuant to which
State has filed counter affidavit.
6. While  learned  counsel  for  the  appellant  prays  for  bail
1

--- Page 270 ---
contending that investigation is complete and the charge-sheet is
filed; therefore there is no requirement for further custody of the
appellant, learned State Counsel on the other hand submits that it
is a drop case in which the demand and acceptance of bribe by the
appellant  has  been  proved.  The  victims  are  poor  villagers.
Presently  sanction  is  awaited  from  the  competent  authority.
Notwithstanding non-receipt of sanction, the trial can proceed
against the appellant as there is no bar for conducting the trial
in the absence of sanction. In such circumstances, it will be
premature to enlarge the appellant on bail.
7. Submissions made by learned counsel for the parties are
received due consideration of the Court.
8. Petitioner is serving as Land Record Inspector in the State of
Rajasthan.  We  refrain  from  entering  into  the  merit  of  the
allegations leveled against the appellant which in any event would
have to be proved in the course of the trial. However, the fact
remains that the appellant is in custody since 28.02.2026and in the
meanwhile,  the  investigation  is  complete  following  which
chargesheet has been filed. In such circumstances, continuing with
the detention of the appellant in our view would be oppressive. We,
therefore, direct that the appellant shall be produced before the
learned special Judge within a maximum period of seven days from
today whereafter the learned Special Judge shall release the
appellant on bail on such terms and conditions as may be due
appropriate.
2

--- Page 271 ---
9. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 14, 2026.
IZ
3

--- Page 272 ---

ITEM NO.36               COURT NO.14               SECTION IX
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  21313/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  13-03-2026 in WP
No. 3374/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay]
SHEKHAR RAJKUMAR GUPTA & ORS.                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
GODREJ FINANCE LIMITED                             Respondent(s)
[ONLY IA NO. 184404/2026 IS LISTED UNDER THIS ITEM] 
IA No. 184404/2026 - APPLICATION FOR VACATION OF INTERIM ORDER
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Eshna Kumar, Adv. (through VC)
                   Ms. Astha Agarwal, Adv.
                   Ms. Kruti Bhavsar, Adv.
                   Mr. Anand Dilip Landge, AOR   
                
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Sunil Fernandes, Sr. Adv. (through VC)
                   Mr. Abhinav Hansaria, AOR
                   Mr. Archit Virmani, Adv.  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The present petition calls in question the correctness of
the order dated  13-03-2026 in Writ Petition No. 3374/2026
passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay. By the said
order, the High Court, by an interim arrangement, stayed the
operation of the order dated 09.03.2026 made by the Debts
Recovery Tribunal, Pune (for short, ‘DRT’) in IA No.785/2026

--- Page 278 ---
2
in SA No.648/2025.
2. The  DRT  had,  by  its  order  of  09.03.2026,  in  IA
No.785/2026 in SA No.648/2025 directed the respondent- Godrej
Finance  Limited to  restore  the  possession  of  the  suit
flat/property  to  the  petitioners  herein  on  or  before
14.03.2026. Certain other directions were also made.
3. The net result of the impugned order was the direction to
hand over the possession stood stayed.
4. The petitioners approached this Court. This Court, during
the Partial Court Working Days on 18.06.2026, while issuing
notice on the Special Leave Petition, stayed the operation of
the order (impugned herein) of the High Court dated 13.03.2026
with an express stipulation that consequently, the order of
DRT dated 09.03.2026 would stand revived. The said order
reads as under:-
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioners.
2. Issue notice, returnable on 04.08.2026.
3. In the meanwhile, there shall be stay of the impugned
order dated 13.03.2026 passed by the High Court of
Bombay in Writ Petition No.3374 of 2026, meaning thereby
that the order dated 09.03.2026 passed by the Debts
Recovery Tribunal, Pune in I.A. No.785 of 2026 in S.A.
No.648 of 2025, would stand revived.”
5. Today, learned counsel for the respondent has come up
with an application to vacate the said interim order of
18.06.2026 (IA No. 184404/2026).
6. We  have  heard   Mr.  Sunil  Fernandes,  learned  Senior

--- Page 279 ---
3
Counsel  for  the  respondent  and  Ms.  Eshna  Kumar,  learned
counsel for the petitioners.
7. The situation today is that the main writ petition is
pending in the High Court. This Court has worked out an
interim arrangement by the order of 18.06.2026 which has been
set out hereinabove. 
8. We are of the opinion that interest of justice would be
served, if the order dated 18.06.2026 made by this Court is
confirmed and is directed to operate till the disposal of the
writ petition No.3374/2026 by the High Court.
9. Ordered accordingly.
10. Consequently,  on  or  before  22 nd July,  2026,  the
respondent- Godrej Finance Limited  shall hand over possession
of the premises in question to the petitioners. We further
direct that pending disposal of the writ petition by the High
Court, the petitioners shall not create any third party rights
in the property in question or create encumbrances in any
manner whatsoever on the property in question.
11. We are told that the writ petition is listed for hearing
on 31.07.2026 before the High Court. We request the High Court
to make an endevour to dispose of the said writ petition
itself on that date or on such other earlier date as the High
Court may deem fit.
12. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petition
is disposed of.

--- Page 280 ---
4
13. Pending application(s), if any shall also stand disposed
of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

Transfer Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  3192/2025
SONAL JAIN                             Petitioner(s)

 O R D E R
1. By way of this petition, the
petitioner  is  seeking  transfer  of
petition being F.C.O.P. No. 1007 of
2025,  titled  “ROHIT  JAIN  VS.  SONAL
JAIN” from  the  I  Additional  Family
Court  Charminar,  Hyderabad  to  the
Family Court, Bengaluru, Karnataka.
2. Heard  learned  counsel
appearing for the parties.
3. In  the  facts  and
circumstances  of  the  case,  and  the
material available before us on record,
the petitioner has made out a ground
for transfer of the present petition
and accordingly, the same is allowed.

--- Page 282 ---
2
4. Hence,  as  prayed  for,
petition being F.C.O.P. No. 1007 of
2025,  titled  “ROHIT  JAIN  VS.  SONAL
JAIN” is directed to be transferred
from  the  I  Additional  Family  Court
Charminar,  Hyderabad  to  the  Family
Court, Bengaluru, Karnataka.
5. Liberty  is  reserved  to  the
respondent to request the Transferee
Court to allow him to appear virtually
to attend the proceedings. If such an
application  is  filed,  the  concerned
Court shall consider the same in the
facts and circumstances of the case and
in accordance with law and direct him
to appear physically only when it is
mandatorily required.
6. The entire record pertaining
to the petition being F.C.O.P. No. 1007
of 2025, shall be transmitted forthwith
by  the  transferor  court  to  the
transferee court.

--- Page 283 ---
3
7. The  transfer  petition  is,
accordingly, allowed.
8. Pending  application(s),  if
any, stand closed.

the respondents.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.   3163   OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.9127/2026)
MUNESH         APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and
learned counsel for the respondent-State of Rajasthan.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by order dated 12.01.2026
passed by the High Court of Rajasthan, Bench at Jaipur
(briefly the ‘High Court’ hereinafter), rejecting the
application filed by the appellant for suspension of
sentence during pendency of the appeal before the High
Court.
4. It is stated that appellant and another (Lala Ram)
have been convicted by the Special Court, Protection of
Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Commission for
Protection  of  Child  Rights  Act,  2005,  No.2,  Bundi,
Rajasthan under Section 376D of Indian Penal Code, 1860
1

--- Page 314 ---
(IPC)  and  sentenced  to  undergo  Rigorous  Imprisonment
(R.I.) for 20 years and fine of Rs.1,00,000/- each, with a
default stipulation.
5. Aggrieved by the aforesaid conviction and sentence,
appellant has preferred appeal before the High Court.
During the pendency of the appeal, appellant preferred an
application for suspension of sentence which was rejected
by the High Court vide the impugned order.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that the
main accused Lala Ram has been granted bail by the High
Court. Therefore, he seeks bail for the appellant on the
ground of parity.
7. On  the  other  hand,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent-State of Rajasthan submits that the victim in
this case is an intellectually challenged woman. During
the Test Identification Parade (in short ‘TIP’), she had
specifically identified the present appellant. On a query
by the Court, she submits that the other convict Lala Ram
was not identified because he was not produced during the
TIP.
8. After hearing learned counsel for the parties and on
due consideration, we are of the view that since co-
convict Lala Ram has been granted bail by the High Court
and there being no distinguishing feature to distinguish
the case of the present appellant from that of Lala Ram
and having regard to the fact that appellant has already
undergone more than five years of incarceration, we are
2

--- Page 315 ---
inclined to suspend the sentence and grant bail to the
appellant.
9. In this connection, we are also fortified in our view
by the findings recorded by the Trial Court at Page-119 of
the Paper Book that since no human semen could be detected
on the victim, no DNA test could be conducted; the medical
report does not reveal any evidence against the accused.
However, these are our tentative views and should not be
construed as any opinion on merit.
10. Consequently,  we  direct  that  the  appellant  be
produced before the Trial Court within a maximum period of
seven days from today whereafter the Trial Court shall
release the appellant on bail on such terms and conditions
as may be deemed appropriate.
11. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
15th JULY, 2026.
AK
3

--- Page 316 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.   3162   OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.10432/2026)
MD. SAHJAD         APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF JHARKHAND & ANR.         RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and
learned counsel for the respondent-State of Jharkhand.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by order dated 12/08.12.2025
passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi (briefly
the ‘High Court’ hereinafter) in Cr. Appeal (DB) No.1017
of 2022, rejecting the prayer for suspension of sentence
and release of the appellant on bail pending consideration
of the Criminal Appeal filed by the appellant before the
High Court.
4. It is stated that appellant has been convicted by the
Court of Addl. Sessions Judge-III, Chatra under Sections
304B/34 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and sentenced to
undergo Rigorous Imprisonment (R.I.) for 10 years.
1

--- Page 318 ---
5. Aggrieved by the aforesaid conviction and sentence,
appellant has preferred appeal before the High Court.
Appeal is of the year 2022 and is pending. During pendency
of  the  appeal,  appellant  preferred  Interlocutory
Application  bearing  I.A.  No.8996  of  2025  seeking
suspension of sentence and grant of bail to him. By the
impugned order, the High Court has rejected the prayer for
suspension of sentence made by the appellant.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that he has
undergone incarceration for a period of more than 04 years
07 months. Appeal will take considerable time for its
final disposal. Therefore, appellant may be enlarged on
bail during pendency of the appeal.
7. Opposing the submission made by learned counsel for
the appellant, learned counsel for the respondent-State of
Jharkhand submits that appellant has been convicted for
the death of his wife as a result of demand for dowry. At
the time of death, the victim wife was pregnant and the
incident had occurred in the matrimonial home. Therefore,
it is not a fit case for suspension of sentence.
8. We have considered the rival submissions and perused
the materials on record. At this stage, we refrain from
entering into the merits of the matter since that would be
decided finally by the Appellate Court. The fact remains
that appeal is of the year 2022 and there is very little
possibility of the appeal being heard in the near future.
In the meanwhile, appellant has completed almost half of
2

--- Page 319 ---
the sentence imposed.
9. That being the position, we are of the view that it
would be in the interest of justice if the sentence is
suspended and the appellant is enlarged on bail during
pendency of the Criminal Appeal before the High Court.
10. Ordered accordingly.
11. Let the appellant be produced before the Trial Court
within  a  maximum  period  of  seven  days  from  today
whereafter the Trial Court shall release the appellant on
bail on such terms and conditions as may be deemed
appropriate.
12. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
15th JULY, 2026.
AK

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NOS. 1868-1871 OF 2026
THE STATE OF MANIPUR & ANR. PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
LONGJAM LOVEDY SINGH & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. These transfer petitions have been moved by the State of
Manipur, seeking transfer of intra-court appeals bearing Writ
Appeal Nos.11-14/2026, which are pending before the High Court of
Manipur. These writ appeals are directed against a common judgment
and order dated 09.04.2026, passed by a learned Single Judge of the
High Court in batch of Writ Petitions. Learned counsel for the
contesting respondents are appearing on Caveat.
2. Learned counsel for the parties jointly inform that besides
the Chief Justice, there are only two Judges in the High Court of
Manipur. One learned Judge is the author of the judgment challenged
in the Writ Appeals whereas the other learned Judge has recused in
during the hearing of the said appeals. That being so, no Division
Bench can be constituted in the High Court of Manipur to hear and
decide the said appeals. The parties are, thus, jointly requesting
to transfer the pending Writ Appeals to the Gauhati High Court.
3. In view of the circumstances explained above and the consent
by learned counsel for the parties, the instant Transfer Petitions
are allowed and Writ Appeal Nos.11-14/2026 pending before the High
Court of Manipur are ordered to be transferred to the High Court of
1

--- Page 322 ---
Gauhati, along with original record of Writ Petition (Civil)
Nos.633 of 2024, 501 of 2024, 486 of 2024, 495 of 2024, 580 of 2025
and 728 of 2024, which were decided by the learned Single Judge
vide judgment and order dated 09.04.2026. Ordered accordingly.
4. Learned counsel for the contesting respondents state on record
that, meanwhile, they will not press the contempt petitions filed
against the Petitioner, which are pending before the Manipur High
Court.
5. The parties are directed to appear before the Gauhati High
Court on 06.08.2026.  
..........................CJI.
       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
    (JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
..........................J.
       (V. MOHANA) 
          
 
NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026
2

                             O R D E R
Heard Mr. Vipin Sanghi, learned Senior counsel for the
petitioner and Mr. Rajiv Shakdher, learned Senior Counsel
for the respondent.
2. Pursuant  to  the  order  passed  by  this  Court  on
25.05.2026,  we  are  informed  that  there  has  been
substantial positive development and the child along with
the parties are seeing a Psychologist, which is showing
positive result.
3. We are further informed that a divorce petition has
been moved by both the parties before the Principal Judge,
Family Court and an application has also been filed before
this Court by the respondent seeking divorce by invoking
power  under  Article  142  of  the  Constitution.  Learned
Senior Counsel for the petitioner submits that he will
respond to the said application. It goes without saying
that it shall always be open to the petitioner to respond
to the said application filed by the respondent, if at all
the need arises. 
4. We observe here that in principle, we totally agree
with the stand taken by learned Senior Counsel for the
parties, but if the said order, if at all, has to be
2

--- Page 327 ---
SLP (C) NO.3712/2025
passed, it can only be after culmination and settlement of
remaining issues to the satisfaction of both the parties.
5. Further, an issue has been raised by learned Senior
Counsel for the respondent that though substantial number
of articles have been returned, but few articles which are
of sentimental value and which according to him, has been
purchased from the personal funds of the respondent, are
still lying with the petitioner, which he may return to
the respondent. A list of such items have been handed over
by learned Senior Counsel for the respondent to learned
Senior Counsel for the petitioner, in the presence of the
petitioner. The stand of the petitioner is that he will
respond to the same after going through the list. Further,
it has been submitted by learned Senior Counsel for the
respondent that the issue with regard to providing an
alternative accommodation in which the child will live
with the respondent-mother, nothing substantial has been
achieved, as no serious efforts have been made to locate
the place, which may be accepted to both the parties.
6. We only request the parties to work seriously on this
issue as this is a major stumbling block.
7. In the aforesaid background, we deem it appropriate
that the arrangement continuing as of now, especially with
regard to the parties visiting the Psychologist along with
the child, the same to be continued for another two
3

--- Page 328 ---
SLP (C) NO.3712/2025
months. 
8. List on 10.09.2026 at 2.00 p.m. as part heard.
9. In  the  meantime,  whatever  interim  arrangement,
especially relating to visitation rights, to continue.
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3206/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.12178/2026)
PADMAVATHY                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF TAMIL NADU & ANR.                         Respondent(s)
 O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The present appeal arises out of FIR No.69/2026 registered at
Central Crime Branch, Tambaram City Police, Chennai under Sections
316(2),  218(4),  336(2),  336(3)  and  61(2)  of  Bharatiya  Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023.
3. Respondent  No.2,  who  is  the  original  complainant,  has
allegedly paid Rs.5 Crores to the appellant for purchase of a
property which was mortgaged by the appellant on assurance that the
mortgage will be released by the petitioner before execution of the
sale deed. 
4. The appellant was granted bail by the Judicial First Class
Magistrate-1, Tambaram, Chennai on condition of deposit of Rs.25
lakhs as undertaken by the learned counsel appearing for the
appellant. 
5. The appellant approached the High Court of Madras with the
allegation that the Advocate appearing for her had made the
undertaking without such instruction. The High Court found that it

--- Page 330 ---
2
was too premature to consider the factual matrix whether the
appellant had given the instruction to the counsel to make the
undertaking as submitted before Court and closed the petition,
directing the learned Magistrate to take an appropriate decision in
accordance with law. The said order is challenged before us. 
6. Heard Mr. P.V. Dinesh, learned Senior Counsel appearing for
the appellant and Mr. Balaji Srinivasan, learned Senior counsel
appearing for Respondent No.2. 
7. Respondent No.2 pointed out that there is suppression of
relevant facts and that subsequently the learned Magistrate court
had cancelled the bail for reason of default of compliance of the
undertaking.  Since  the  bail  has  already  been  cancelled,  the
challenge against the impugned Judgment of the High Court fails, is
the contention.         
8. On the other hand, learned Senior Counsel for the appellant
submits that the High Court ought not to have remanded the matter
for  fresh  consideration  since  the  Magistrate  could  not  have
reviewed its own order.    
9. In any event, as of now, the allegation of cheating arose from
a sale agreement which did not fructify. Necessarily for recovery
of money the appellant has to approach the Civil Court which has
already been done. We do not make any comments on the criminal
proceedings initiated since the jurisdictional court is seized of
the matter. However, we are of the opinion that considering the
nature of the allegations, the appellant can be released on bail,
on appearance before the Court of learned Judicial First Class

--- Page 331 ---
3
Magistrate No.1, Tambaram, Chennai without imposing any condition
for deposit of money.  
10. It is made clear that the contentions of both parties shall be
left open and any suppression, as alleged by the respondent, can be
urged before the Magistrate’s Court at the time of trial. 
11. The appellant is ordered to be released on bail, subject to
such other terms and conditions as may be deemed fit and proper to
impose by the Court of the learned Judicial First Class Magistrate
No.1, Tambaram, Chennai. To facilitate the same, the impugned Order
of the High Court and that of the Judicial First Class Magistrate’s
Court dated 20.04.2026 and 04.07.2026 are set aside. 
12 With  the  aforesaid,  the  appeal  stands  disposed  of.
13. Pending applications, if any, shall also stand disposed of.
…………………………………………J     
(J.B. PARDIWALA)
….…………………………………………J
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
15th JULY, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL No. 8287 OF 2026
 UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              APPELLANTS
                                VERSUS
MOHD. MAHIBUR RAHMAN                               RESPONDENT
   
O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the parties.
2. Our attention has been drawn by learned counsel for the appellants
to Section 115 of the Border Security Force Act, 1968 as well as Rules
160 and 161 of the Border Security Force Rules, 1969, to justify the
order of dismissal from service and imprisonment impugned in the writ
petition by the respondent.
3. These statutory provisions, while  circumscribing the power of the
Deputy  Inspector  General 1 of  the  Border  Security  Force 2 to  whom
proceedings of a Summary Security Force Court3 are forwarded, appears
to have been conceived for the benefit of the member of the Force
proceeded against. The DIG is authorised, upon recording a satisfaction
that injustice has been caused to the member in course of the SSFC
proceedings,  to  either  set  aside  the  proceedings  or  to  reduce  the
penalty  awarded  in  pursuance  of  such  proceedings  by  recording
1  DIG
2  Force 
3  SSFC

--- Page 338 ---
2
reasons. However, in terms thereof, the DIG has no power to convene a
General  Security  Force  Court  to  inquire  into  new/fresh  charges  for
imposition of a punishment higher than the one which was imposed
upon such member pursuant to SSFC proceedings. 
4. The Division Bench of the Jammu and Kashmir High Court was right
in  its  interference  with  the  order  of  dismissal  from  service  and
imprisonment, impugned in the writ petition.
5. There is no merit in this appeal, which stands dismissed.
6. Since the respondent has attained the age of superannuation and
cannot be reinstated in service, the appellants are directed to act in
terms of the impugned judgment and order and release all financial
benefits to him within 3 (three) months.
7. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………….............................J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]
……………..............................J.
[SHEEL NAGU]
New Delhi;
July 15, 2026.

3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.259 OF 2026
PANKAJ KUMAR MEHTA                      PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
ANJU BALA                              RESPONDENT(S)
 WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 296 OF 2026 
O R D E R
1. These transfer petitions have been filed by the
petitioner-husband seeking transfer of proceedings in
125 Cr.P.C./117/2024 titled “Anju Bala vs. Pankaj Kumar
Mehta” pending before the Additional Principal Judge-I,
Family Court, Solan, Himachal Pradesh and in Domestic
Violence  Act./22/2025,  titled  “Anju  Bala  vs.  Pankaj
Kumar”  pending  before  the  Additional  Chief  Judicial
Magistrate, Solan, Himachal Pradesh to the Principal
Judge,  Family  Court,  Chandigarh  and  Chief  Judicial
Magistrate, Chandigarh respectively.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties and perused
the material placed on record.
3. We have considered the facts of these cases and
grounds on which transfer has been sought.  On such
consideration, we are satisfied with the grounds urged
by the petitioner and accept the same. Therefore, we
direct that both the aforementioned cases be transferred
1

--- Page 356 ---
to the District & Sessions Judge, District Chandigarh,
who may try these cases himself or assign them to a
Court of competent jurisdiction.
4. Records  of  these  cases  should  immediately  be
transferred to the transferee court.
5. The  parties  may  explore  the  possibility  of
settlement through Mediation. The transferee court would
obtain the desire and wishes of the parties and proceed
accordingly in this regard.
6. The  parties  are  at  liberty  initially  to  appear
through virtual mode. If such request is made, the
concerned court may favourably consider the same and
thereafter, if need be, the parties can be asked to
appear through physical mode.
7. The Transfer Petitions are accordingly allowed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
   (SANDEEP MEHTA)
  NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026.

--- Page 359 ---
2
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1.  Ms. Nisha Priya Bhatiya, Advocate has placed before the Court
a letter dated 14.07.2026 and requests the Court to peruse the
same. Advocate Ms. Nisha Priya Bhatiya, is fair enough to the
limited extent of saying that she prefers not to read the letter
handed across the bar to the Court. However, she desires that the
Court go through the letter.
2.  We have, as has been requested by the counsel, gone through
the letter. The letter is dated 14.07.2026 and refers to a few
interactions that occurred during the hearing on 24.11.2025 in this
Court while disposing of SLP(Crl) No. 15849/2025.  We take note of
the contents of the letter. We appreciate the anxiety of the
counsel and her client. Without observing anything further, we
clearly and categorically reject the request made through the
letter dated 14.07.2026.
3. After the order is dictated and the view the Court has taken
on the letter dated 14.07.2026, Advocate Nisha Priya Bhatiya
expresses unconditional apology and withdraws every allegation made
in the letter dated 14.07.2026 and requests the Court to hear both
the applications listed before the Court. 
4. We understand the anxiety and the circumstances in which
Advocate Nisha Priya Bhatiya might have filed the letter dated
14.07.2026.  Since  she  has  expressed  remorse  in  the  Court,
apologized and reaffirmed her faith in the Court to take up the
applications, the Court does not prefer to take the circumstances

--- Page 360 ---
3
seriously. 
5. The controversy in the letter may be treated as closed.
Registry is directed to keep the letter filed in a sealed cover.
6. At  the  request  of  Mr.  K.M.  Nataraj,  learned  Additional
Solicitor General for filing counter affidavit, list the matters on
17.07.2026.
    (LOKESH ARORA)                              (NIDHI MATHUR)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)

MANJIT KUMAR SINGH ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
As per the office report, the respondent has been served.
However, he has chosen not to enter appearance.  The respondent is,
therefore, set ex parte.
Having given due consideration to the contentions raised by
the petitioner and the averments made in the transfer petition, the
transfer petition is allowed in the interest of justice. 
Accordingly,  Matrimonial Case No. 7328/2025, titled " Manjit
Kumar Singh vs. Smt. Rawat Bhumika Gajendrasingh ", pending before
the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Bengaluru, Karnataka, is
directed to be transferred to the Presiding Officer, Family Court,
Vadodara, Gujarat, who may hear the case himself/herself or assign
it to a Court of competent jurisdiction.
Records of the case shall be immediately transferred to the
transferee Court.
To cut short delay, parties are directed to appear before the
transferee court on 17.08.2026. On the said date and thereafter,
the parties may appear in person or through their authorized
1

--- Page 365 ---
representatives or through video-conferencing, if the said facility
is available in the transferee Court and is permitted by it. In
case there is non-appearance on behalf of any party on the date
fixed by this Court, notice shall be issued by the transferee Court
to the defaulting party.  
The parties may explore the possibility of settlement through
mediation. The transferee Court may obtain the desire and wishes of
the parties and proceed accordingly in this regard.
 The transferee Court shall decide the matter expeditiously.
Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.

                           O R D E R
1. Exemption applications are allowed.
2. By way of this petition filed under Section 25 CPC read
with  Order  XLI  of  the  Supreme  Court  Rules,  2013,  the
petitioner - wife seeks transfer of the Matrimonial Case
No.1611 of 2024 titled as “Ankit Singh vs. Swati Singh”, filed
by the respondent – husband, pending in the Court of Principal
Judge, Family Courts, Gautam Buddha Nagar, Uttar Pradesh to
the  Court  of  jurisdiction  in  the  South  District,  Saket
District Courts, New Delhi.
3.  Sufficient cause having been made out by the petitioner –
wife, warranting transfer of the case, as prayed for by her,
the Transfer Petition deserves to be allowed. It is ordered
accordingly.
4.  Consequently, the Matrimonial Case No.1611 of 2024 titled
as “ Ankit Singh vs. Swati Singh ”, pending in the Court of
Principal Judge, Family Courts, Gautam Buddha Nagar, Uttar
Pradesh  is  ordered  to  be  transferred  to  the  Court  of
jurisdiction in the South District, Saket District Courts, New
Delhi.
1

--- Page 368 ---
5. The entire record pertaining to the said case shall be
transmitted  forthwith  by  the  transferor  court  to  the
transferee court.
6. The transferee court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which for an early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.
7. The respondent is permitted to attend the proceedings
before the transferee court online, except on such date(s)
when his personal presence is mandatorily required by the
Court.
8. Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed of.
  ………………………………………………J.

       ………………………………………………J.

IA No. 94091/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Devvrat, Adv.
                   Mr. Shivam Singh, Adv.

                   Ms. Ritu Reniwal, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Devvrat,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner  and  Mr.  Samir  Ali  Khan,  learned  counsel
appearing for the respondent-State.
SLP (Criminal) No.9239/2026              Page 1 of 3

--- Page 377 ---
2. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the
correctness of the order dated 10 th October, 2025 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Patna, Bihar in
Criminal Miscellaneous No.35053 of 2025 whereby the bail
application of the petitioner has been rejected.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
petitioner has already undergone 3 years and 5 months in
custody, since he was arrested on 21 st February, 2023.
Learned  counsel  drawing  attention  to  the  First
Information Report submits that while the petitioner is
named in the first part of the incident which did not
cause injury to any person and only a reference to
indiscriminate firing was made, there is no reference to
the petitioner in the second part of the incident which
resulted in the death of four persons.  Learned counsel
also  draws  attention  to  the  fact  that  identically
situated accused are on bail.  Learned counsel further
submits that Bachcha Rai whose name figures in the second
part of the incident is also on bail.
4. Mr.  Samir  Ali  Khan,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent-State vehemently defends the impugned order
and submits that out of 9 witnesses, 6 are already
examined.  In response, Mr. Devvrat, learned counsel for
the petitioner submits that the trial is unlikely to
conclude soon.
SLP (Criminal) No.9239/2026              Page 2 of 3

--- Page 378 ---
5. Keeping in view the overall facts and circumstances
of  the  case  and,  particularly,  the  fact  that  the
identically situated accused are on bail, we are inclined
to enlarge the petitioner on bail, pending trial.
6. We direct that the petitioner shall be enlarged on
bail subject to the satisfaction of the Trial Court,
pending trial.
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
8. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

appellant-Ram  Pratap  is  physically  incapable  of
performing daily chores by himself.

subject to the satisfaction of the Trial Court.  The

ITEM NO.5               COURT NO.14               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  8624/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 25-03-2026
in BA No. 658/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay]
MD. MONAZIR HASSAN                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE UNION OF INDIA & ANR.                          Respondent(s)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Sharad Raghav, Adv.
                   Mr. Deepak Goel, AOR
                   Ms. Alka Goyal, Adv.
                   Mr. Shailesh Sharma, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
No.1               Mr. Davinder Pal Singh, A.S.G.
                   Ms. Aastha Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Disha Thakkar, Adv.
                   Mr. B.K.Satija, Adv.
                   Mr. Vikash Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
                   Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AOR                   
                   
For respondent No.2
                   Ms. Ira Mahajan, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Dharmadhikari, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, AOR
                   Mr. Shrirang B. Varma, Adv.  
SLP (Criminal) No.8624 of 2026 Page 1 of  3

--- Page 386 ---
                 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Sharad Raghav, learned counsel appearing
for the petitioner and Mr. Davinder Pal Singh, learned
Additional Solicitor General appearing for respondent
No.1-Union of India.
2. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
question the correctness of the order dated 25 th March,
2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay in
Bail Application No.658 of 2025. By the said order, the
petitioner has been denied the benefit of regular bail in
connection with NDPS Case No.1071 of 2023 arising out of
File No.NCB/MZU/CR-08/2023 under Sections 8(c), 22(c),
28, 27A & 29 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic
Substances Act, 1985 (for short, “the Act”).
3. Learned counsel submits that the petitioner has
already completed 3 years in custody.  Learned counsel
further submits that the petitioner is a licenced dealer
in drugs including of the contraband and he has been
roped in on account of confession of the co-accused.
4. Learned Additional Solicitor General appearing for
respondent No.1 vehemently opposed the submission and
submits that the petitioner has no licence to transport
the  drug.   Learned  ASG  further  submits  that  CCTV
footages, courier records, CDR and bank records are
SLP (Criminal) No.8624 of 2026 Page 2 of  3

--- Page 387 ---
there.
5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
perused the records.
6. The fact remains that the petitioner has already
put in 3 years.  It is not disputed that at least 15
witnesses are  yet to  be examined  on behalf  of the
prosecution.
7. Considering the overall nature of the matter and
the fact that the petitioner has already undergone 3
years in custody coupled with the fact that the trial is
expected to  take a  long time  and factoring  in the
principles in Section 37 of the Act, we are of the
opinion that the petitioner should be enlarged on bail,
pending trial.
8. We direct that pending trial, the petitioner shall
be enlarged on bail subject to the satisfaction of the
Trial Court.  
9. It  is  made  clear  that  the  petitioner  shall,
however, not in any manner delay the conclusion of the
trial. 
10. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
11. Pending  application,  if  any,  shall  also  stand
disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
SLP (Criminal) No.8624 of 2026 Page 3 of  3

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 17-04-2026
in BA No. 794/2026 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi]
BADRUDDIN MIYAN                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND                             Respondent(s)
(IA No. 145845/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Ms. Jaikriti S. Jadeja, AOR
                   Ms. Neha Tyagi, Adv.
                   Ms. Nidhi Singh, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Jayant Mohan, AOR
                   Ms. Adya Shree Dutta, Adv.
                   Ms. Dorjee Ongmu Lachungpa, Adv.
                   Mr. Priyanshu Teotia, Adv.                  
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Ms.  Jaikriti  S.Jadeja,  learned  counsel
appearing  for  the  petitioner  and  Mr.  Jayant  Mohan,
learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State.
SLP (Crl.) No.8807/2026 Page 1 of 3

--- Page 389 ---
2. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
question the correctness of the order dated 17 th April,
2026 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi in
Bail Application No.794 of 2026. By the said order, the
petitioner has been denied the benefit of regular bail in
connection NDPS Case No.37 of 2025 arising out of FIR
No.46 of 2024 under Sections 17(c), 18(b) and 22(c) of
the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985
(for short, “the Act”).
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
petitioner has already undergone approximately 10 months
in custody.  Learned counsel further submits that the
petitioner has been roped in on the confession of the
co-accused-Dharmendra Prasad Sao from whom the contraband
has been seized.  It is further submitted that taking the
case of the prosecution at the highest, the case is that
the co-accused Dharmendra Prasad Sao proposed to sell the
contraband to the petitioner at a certain rate.
4. Having  Considered  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances; the period of custody already undergone,
prima  facie  nature  of  the  prosecution  case;  the
likelihood of the trial being prolonged and keeping in
mind the ingredients of Section 37 of the Act, we are of
the  opinion  that  the  petitioner  is  entitled  to  be
enlarged on bail, pending trial.
SLP (Crl.) No.8807/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 390 ---
5. We direct that pending trial, the petitioner shall
be enlarged on bail subject to the satisfaction of the
Trial Court.  
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
7. Pending application shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

ITEM NO.20               COURT NO.1               SECTION X
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Writ Petition(s)(Civil) No(s).737/2026
NISHANT KHATRI                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              Respondent(s)
IA No. 169166/2026 - PERMISSION TO APPEAR AND ARGUE IN PERSON, IA
No. 169163/2026 - REFERENCE TO A LARGER BENCH, IA No. 169165/2026 -
STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) :  Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Permission to appear and argue in-person is granted.
2. The  instant  writ  petition,  under  Article  32  of  the
Constitution of India, has been filed seeking following reliefs:
“1. To Issue a writ of Mandamus And/Or certiorari or any
other appropriate writ, order or direction declaring the
Constitution  (Eighty-Fourth  Amendment)  Act,  2001  and
Constitution  (Eighty-Seventh  Amendment)  Act,  2003  as
unconstitutional, being violative of the Basic Structure
of  the  Constitution,  inasmuch  as  it  distorts
representative democracy, political equality, and free
and fair elections;
2. To Issue a writ of Mandamus to declare that Article
55, 81, 82, 170, 330 and 332 of the Constitution of
India are Unconstitutional and violative of the Basic
Structure of the Constitution insofar as they mandate or
permit reliance on population figures of 1971 and/or
2001.
1

--- Page 392 ---
3. To Issue a writ of Mandamus to declare that Sections
4, 8, and 9 of the Delimitation Commission Act, 2002,
insofar as they mandate or permit reliance on population
figures  of  1971  and  2001  for  the  purpose  of
delimitation,  are  unconstitutional  and  violative  of
Articles 14, 81, 170 and the Basic Structure of the
Constitution;
4. To Issue a writ of Mandamus to declare that the
conduct  of  Special  Intensive  Revision  (SIR)  under
Section 21 of the Representation of the People Act,
1950, without prior apportionment and delimitation based
on the latest census, is unconstitutional, arbitrary,
and violative of Articles 14, 81, 170 and the Basic
Structure of the Constitution;
5. Issue a writ of mandamus or any other appropriate
direction directing the Respondents to undertake fresh
apportionment and delimitation of constituencies for the
House of the People and State Legislative Assemblies
strictly in accordance with the latest published census
data;
6. To Issue a writ of Mandamus to declare that the
continued  reliance  on  outdated  census  data  for
determining  representation  is  unconstitutional,  being
violative of the principles of political equality, equal
representation, and democratic fairness;
7.  To  Issue  a  writ  of  Mandamus  for  appropriate
directions to ensure that electoral processes, including
preparation and revision of electoral rolls, are carried
out  in  a  manner  consistent  with  constitutionally
mandated representation, so as to maintain coherence
between population data, voter rolls, and legislative
representation;
8. To Issue a writ of Mandamus to direct that no
elections to the Lok Sabha or to any State Legislative
Assembly  be  conducted  unless  and  until  such
constitutionally  compliant  apportionment  and
delimitation exercise is carried out;
9. Issue a writ of mandamus or any other appropriate
direction directing Respondent No. 1 (Union of India) to
forthwith constitute a Delimitation Commission for the
purpose  of  carrying  out  fresh  apportionment  and
delimitation of constituencies for the House of the
People and State Legislative Assemblies in accordance
with the latest published census data, so as to ensure
compliance with Articles 81 and 170 of the Constitution
and to restore equality in representation.
10. Pass such further or other orders, directions, or
2

--- Page 393 ---
writs as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in
the facts and circumstances of the present case, in the
interest of justice, equity, and to uphold the Basic
Structure of the Constitution.”
3. We have heard Mr. Nishant Khatri, petitioner-in-person, at a
considerable length.
4. It seems to us that the reliefs sought in the instant petition
essentially involve formulation, revision, and/or amendment of the
Constitutional  and  legislative  policy.  This  Court  may  not,
therefore, be an appropriate platform for the aforesaid purpose at
this stage.
5. The petitioner, if so advised, may approach the Committee on
Petitions, constituted under Rule 306 of the Rules of Procedure and
Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha, by way of a comprehensive
petition.
6. Similarly, the petitioner shall be at liberty to send a copy
of this writ petition, as a representation, to the respondents as
well as other concerned Authorities.
7. Given the liberty aforementioned, the petitioner-in-person
seeks and is permitted to withdraw this petition. The Writ Petition
is accordingly disposed of as withdrawn.
8. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
(ARJUN BISHT)                             (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
3

  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No(s).6533-6560/2026
