PRINCIPAL COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS (PORT)          APPELLANT(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S ITC LIMITED                                RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The learned counsel for the appellant fairly states that the
Customs  Excise  Service  Tax  Appellate  Tribunal  (CESTAT)  while
passing the impugned order,  inter alia , relied on its earlier
decision in M/s. Viraj Profiles Ltd. v. Commissioner of Customs
(Preventive) Mumbai, which has been upheld by this Court vide order
dated  01.04.2024,  reported  as  “ Commissioner  of  Customs
(Preventive), Mumbai vs. Viraj Profiles Ltd.” 2024 (388) E.L.T. 673
(SC) :2024 SCC OnLine SC 3670, and therefore, the issue stands
settled against the Revenue.
3. In view of the above, the appeals are dismissed.
4. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Civil Appeal No(s).6533-6560/2026
PRINCIPAL COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS (PORT)           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S ITC LIMITED                                    Respondent(s)
IA No. 106542/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 106554/2026 - EX-PARTE STAY
 
Date : 24-04-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Appellant(s) : 
                   Mr. N. Venkataraman, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Gurmeet Singh Makker, AOR
                   Mr. V.C. Bharathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Ishaan Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Varad A Kilor, Adv.
                   Ms. Snehashish Mukherjee, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The appeals are dismissed in terms of the signed order.

TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.3090/2025
KALA     PETITIONER
                                VERSUS
SATISH CHANDRA     RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed under Section 25 of the
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”) by the petitioner
seeking transfer of HMA No.2203/2025, titled as "Satish Chandra Vs.
Kala", pending before the Principal Judge, Family Court, South-
West,  Dwarka  Court,  Delhi  to  the  Competent  Court,  Ramnagar,
District – Nainital, Uttarakhand. 
2. Having gone through the material available on record and also
the facts of the case, we are of the view that this Transfer
Petition deserves to be allowed. Accordingly, HMA No.2203/2025,
titled as "Satish Chandra Vs. Kala", pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, South-West, Dwarka Court, Delhi is transferred
to the Competent Court, Ramnagar, District – Nainital, Uttarakhand.
3. Records shall be sent by the Court where proceedings are
pending to the transferee Court promptly.
4. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned courts for
compliance.
1

--- Page 407 ---
5. The Transfer Petition stands allowed in the aforesaid terms.
6. On an application made by the parties, the Court to which the
case is transferred shall permit the parties to appear through
video conference on all dates except on the dates on which their
personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

--- Page 413 ---
2
         Mr. Aman Panwar, AAG
    Mr. Sanchit Garga, AoR
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.
2. In  compliance  of  our  previous  order  dated  29.05.2026,
Mr. Jayamadhava P., Special Deputy Commissioner, Bengaluru Urban
District, Bengaluru, Karnataka has filed an affidavit indicating
that steps have been taken under the Karnataka Land Revenue Act,
1964.
3. From the said affidavit filed, it has been pointed out that
residential land in the name of wife of respondent no.5 has been
identified and first notice of recovery was issued on 06.07.2026
and today, the second notice was also issued and it will follow
with attachment and thereafter, the forfeited property will be
auctioned to recover the amount.
4. However, in view of there being no clarity on the valuation of
the  flat  which  tentatively,  according  to  learned  Additional
Advocate General for the State of Karnataka, may not meet the
demand of Rs.50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs), the authorities
would also take action with regard to other movable/immovable
properties, including bank account(s) etc.
5. At this juncture, we were informed that Mr. Jayamadhava P.,
Special Deputy Commissioner, Bengaluru Urban District, Bengaluru,
Karnataka, who has affirmed the affidavit, has also joined the
proceeding through video conferencing. We have interacted with him.

--- Page 414 ---
3
When asked, as to why, he had not initiated any action with regard
to immovable properties, including the bank account(s) of the
respondent  no.5  and  why  such  fact  with  regard  to  the  bank
account(s) is not disclosed in the affidavit, he had no answer and
only expressed his apology. However, it was assured, both by him
and the learned Additional Advocate General for the State of
Karnataka, that appropriate and complete action shall be taken to
ensure that the cost of Rs.50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs), as
directed is recovered from the respondent no.5.
6. Having regard to the aforesaid, by way of last indulgence, the
matter be listed on 31.07.2026 at 02:00 p.m.
7. It is made clear that whatever amount is recovered in excess
of Rs.50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs) would not be paid or
returned  to  the  respondent  no.5  and  the  Special  Deputy
Commissioner, Bengaluru Urban District, Bengaluru, Karnataka, shall
abide by further directions to be issued on the next date by this
Court. We make it clear that all efforts would be made to recover
the maximum amount from the respondent no.5.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.

--- Page 416 ---
2
2. The parties are also present in the Court duly identified by
their learned counsels.
3. At  the  outset,  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent  no.2
submitted that due to some communication gap, it was represented
that the petitioner was not ready to go to her matrimonial home in
terms of the earlier direction passed by this Court on 26.02.2026,
which probably was erroneous. It was submitted that when the
respondent no.2 had gone to the house of the petitioner, he had
requested her to reside in the village home of the respondent no.2
for four months till the time the respondent no.2 made arrangements
in the State of Assam where he is working to take her there.
4. We are not convinced with the stand taken by the learned
counsel for the respondent no.2.
5. However, in view of the categorical stand taken today by the
learned counsel for the respondent no.2 that respondent no.2 will
take the petitioner to the place where he resides and shall live
with her and shall also support her in the way a husband is
required to support his wife, we are inclined to show indulgence.
6. At this juncture, learned counsel for the petitioner submitted
that there was a further direction passed by this Court on
26.02.2026 to the respondent no.2 to pay Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two
Lakhs) to the petitioner, which also has not been complied with.
7. On a response sought from learned counsel for the respondent
no.2, the stand is that the same shall also be paid within one
week.
8. Having regard to the aforesaid, as represented by the learned
counsel for the respondent no.2 in the presence of the respondent

--- Page 417 ---
3
no.2, let respondent no.2 take the petitioner with him to the place
where he is actually working. The couple will live together. The
amount of Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs) be transferred into the
bank account of the petitioner by the respondent no.2 within two
weeks  from  today.  The  respondent  no.2  shall  also  take  the
petitioner with him within the same period.
9. It is made clear that all expenses, i.e., cost of living,
clothing, etc., of the petitioner shall be the responsibility of
the respondent no.2.
10. List on 22.09.2026 at the top of the Board.
11. On the next date of listing, the parties need not be present
in Court keeping in mind the fact that they would be residing in
the State of Assam.
12. After  the  order  was  dictated,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner requested the Court to fix a particular date when the
respondent no.2 would come to the house of the petitioner and take
her with him.
13. On a query from the learned counsel for the respondent no.2,
it is submitted that the respondent no.2 shall convey the exact
date when he will go to the house of the petitioner, within two
days from today, both to the petitioner as well as to the learned
counsel for the petitioner. Let the same be done.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.           OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(C) Nos.14481-14482/2026)
M/S DAAJ HOTELS AND RESORTS PVT. 
LTD 
..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
M/S MFAR CONSTRUCTIONS PRIVATE 
LIMITED
..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is aggrieved by the dismissal of its application
for condonation of the delay of 42 days in preferring an
appeal under Section 37 of the Arbitration and Conciliation
Act, 1996 1, against the order dated 11.02.2025 passed in
C.O.P. No. 33/2018 by the learned Principal District Judge for
Trial and Disposal of Commercial Disputes, Hyderabad, under
Section 34 of the 1996 Act.
3. By the impugned common judgment dated 03.03.2026, a Division
Bench of the High Court for the State of Telangana at
Hyderabad opined that sufficient cause was not shown for the
delay and, accordingly, dismissed the application. 
4.  We may note that the delay in the filing of the appeal was 42
days. In the light of the law laid down by this Court in
Government of Maharashtra (Water Resources Department) v. M/s
Borse Brothers Engineers & Contractors Pvt. Ltd. 2, and given
1 for short "1996 Act"
2  2021 6 SCC 460

--- Page 431 ---
the length of the delay in the filing of the appeal, we are of
the opinion that this was not a case where the appellant ought
to have been non-suited on the ground of that delay. However,
as it is evident that sufficient cause was not shown in
explicit terms in the application filed for condonation of the
delay, indicating a rather casual approach to the matter, we
are of the opinion that the appellant is liable to pay costs. 
5. The appeals are, accordingly, allowed condoning the delay of
42 days in the filing of the appeal. The appeal shall be
entertained and disposed of on merits and in accordance with
law. 
6. The appellant shall pay costs of 2,00,000/- (Rupees two lakhs₹
only) to the respondent within a period of two weeks from
today. 
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
.....................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
.....................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15, 2026.

O R D E R
1. The  petitioner-wife  has  filed  the  petition  under
Section 25 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before
this Court seeking transfer of Case bearing C.P.Petition
No. 01 of 2025  titled as “Rajesh Bissoyi Vs. Roshni
Bissoyi” pending before the Family Court at Nabarangpur,
Orissa to the Family Court at Bastar, Chhattisgarh.
2. Office Report dated 13.07.2026 indicates that despite
service is complete on the sole respondent, no one has
entered appearance on his behalf.  
3. Having  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner and taking into consideration the grounds taken
in the transfer petition, we allow the transfer petition
and direct the transfer of case bearing C.P.Petition No.
01 of 2025 titled as “Rajesh Bissoyi Vs. Roshni Bissoyi”
from the Family Court at Nabarangpur, Orissa to the Family
Court at Bastar, Chhattisgarh.
4. Let the record of the case be transferred without
delay.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

R. SANDHYA                                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
AHKSHAEY RAVI                                   Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. The  petitioner-wife  has  filed  the  petition  under
Section 25 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before
this Court seeking transfer of Case bearing M.C.No. 1245
of 2025 titled as “Ahkshaey Ravi vs. R. Sandhya” pending
before the Family Court at Bangalore to the Family Court
at Chennai, Tamil Nadu.  
2. Vide order dated 26.11.2025, by the consent of the
parties,  the  matter  was  referred  to  Supreme  Court
Mediation Centre.  The Mediation Report dated 25.03.2026
indicates that the mediation between the parties has
failed. 
3. Heard learned counsel appearing for the petitioner and
the respondent.  Learned counsel for the respondent has
vehemently  opposed  the  Transfer  Petition.   However,
taking  into  consideration  the  grounds  taken  in  the
transfer petition, we are of the opinion that interest of
justice would be subserved if we allow the transfer
petition.  Hence, we direct that the case bearing M.C.No.
1245 of 2025 titled as “Ahkshaey Ravi vs. R. Sandhya” be

--- Page 437 ---
2
transferred from the Family Court at Bangalore to the
Family Court at Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
4. Let the record of the case be transferred without
delay.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court
for compliance.
6. If video conferencing facility is available with the
transferee  Court,  the  benefit  of  the  same  shall  be
extended to the parties.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.2435 OF 2025
NIBHA JAISWAL                              PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
SONU JAISWAL                               RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the respondent, at the outset,
has drawn the attention of this Court to the order dated
26.02.2026 passed in T.P. (Civil) No.3152 of 2025,
whereby the Transfer Petition filed by the petitioner-
wife was dismissed by this Court in respect of Case No.
21/2025 titled "Sonu Jaiswal vs. Nibha Jaiswal", pending
before the Court of the Learned Additional District
Judge-I, Chinsurah, Hooghly, West Bengal, wherein the
custody of the children was involved.
2. Learned counsel for the respondent has submitted
that the petitioner-wife is regularly attending the
aforesaid case and that the respondent-husband is ready
to  bear  the  travelling  expenses.  He  has  further
submitted that the distance between the two places is
hardly 250 km.
3. After hearing learned counsel for the parties,
this Court is of the opinion that the petitioner-wife is

--- Page 446 ---
2
certainly entitled to expenses for attending proceedings
in Matrimonial Suit No.34 of 2025, titled "Sonu Jaiswal
vs. Nibha Jaiswal", pending before the Court of the 2nd
ADJ, FTC, Chinsurah, Hooghly, West Bengal. Resultantly,
the respondent-husband is directed to pay a sum of Rs.
10,000/- (Rupees Ten Thousand only) on each and every
date of hearing in Matrimonial Suit No. 34 of 2025. No
case for transfer is made out. The Trial Court is
directed to club both the aforesaid matters and to
assign a common date of hearing in both matters whenever
they are listed before the Trial Court.
4.  The Transfer Petition is, accordingly, dismissed.
5.  Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of. 
….………………………………………………J.
    [SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
…………..….………………………………………………J.
[NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
  
NEW DELHI
13th July, 2026

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  22-04-2024
in MFA No. 2521/2024 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Bengaluru]
M/S SAMMYS DREAM LAND CO. PVT. LTD.                PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
KEMPANNA & ORS.                                    RESPONDENT(S)
(FOR ADMISSION and IA No.203133/2024-EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. 
IA No. 4335/2026 - APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION
IA No. 203133/2024 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 164558/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES)
 
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Sajan Poovayya, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Lzafeer Ahmad B. F., AOR
                   Ms. Raksha Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Palash Maheshwari, Adv.
                   Mr. Shubhamarun, Adv.
                   Ms. Sindura N Swamy, Adv.
                   Mr. Pawan Kant Singh, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Jayant K. Mehta, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Prashant L. Kanal, Adv.
                   Ms. Manasvini Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Sajal Jain, AOR
                                      
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition is arising out of impugned
final judgment and order dated 22.04.2024 passed by the High Court
of Karnataka at Bangalore in Miscellaneous First Appeal No.2521 of
2024.

--- Page 449 ---
2
2. The brief facts of case reveal that in a civil suit, an
application was preferred under Order 39 Rules 1 and 2 of CPC and
the same was allowed restraining the defendants therein from
creating third party rights over the suit property. An appeal was
preferred by the defendant No.3-respondent No.1 herein before the
High Court and the High Court has declined to interfere with the
order, however, the High Court has further ordered that s tatus quo
in respect of the suit property be maintained pending disposal of
the suit. The grievance of the petitioner is that on account of the
order of status quo the project which was going on over the land in
question has come to a stand still and the petitioner suffering
great financial loss. On the other hand,  Mr. Jayant K. Mehta,
learned senior counsel has vehemently argued before this Court that
the title of the suit property is in favour of the respondents and,
therefore, the order of  status quo  was rightly passed in the
matter.
3. This Court after hearing learned counsel for the parties at
length is of the opinion that the order passed by the High Court
deserves to be modified and it is modified accordingly. The present
petitioner will not create any third party right in respect of the
suit property nor will exploit the suit property commercially till
the civil suits are decided finally as already ordered by the High
Court. Both these civil suits have been clubbed together and the
trial court shall make all possible endeavour to decide the civil
suits in question within a period of six months from today.

--- Page 450 ---
3
4.  With aforesaid, the special leave petition stands disposed
of.
5.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.    
(JAGDISH KUMAR)                                 (KOMAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

allowed. Accordingly, M.P. No. 2100/2024 titled as

to  the  Court  at  Kotwali,  District  Haridwar,

IA No. 232395/2023 - APPLICATION FOR SUBSTITUTION

--- Page 481 ---
 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRL) NO. 60 OF 2025
GYANADIPTA ROUT         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
RASHMI ROUT          Respondent(s)
O R D E R
This  is  a  petition  filed  under  Section  446  of  the
Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.  
The petitioner has filed this transfer petition seeking
the following reliefs:
“a. Transfer the Complaint Case No.1103 of 2024
titled as “Rashmi Rout  Vs. Gyanadipta Rout & Ors.”
pending before the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate,
Saket and Maintenance Petition No.198/2024 tilted as
“Rashmi Rout  Vs. Gyanadipta Rout”  pending before
Principal Family Court, Saket, New Delhi to Sub-
Divisional Judicial Magistrate, Koraput, Odisha.
b. Pass such other and further order or direction
that this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and appropriate
in the facts and circumstances of the case.”
We have heard learned counsel for the respective parties.
During the course of submission, it was brought to our
notice that the respondent/wife had filed Transfer Petition
(C) No.2468/2024 before this court seeking transfer of matter
which   had  been  filed by the petitioner/husband before the
contd..

--- Page 482 ---
- 2 -
Civil Judge, Senior Division, Koraput, Odisha and the said
transfer petition was allowed and the matter was transferred
to Principal Judge, Family Court, Saket, New Delhi.
Therefore, having regard to the consistency in the venue
of  the  cases,  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent/wife
submitted that this Court may not grant the transfer of the
aforesaid cases pending before the Court of Chief Metropolitan
Magistrate and Family Court, Saket, New Delhi to the Sub-
Divisional Judicial Magistrate, Koraput, Odisha. 
We have considered the submissions advanced at the Bar.
We do not find any reason to grant the relief sought for
by the petitioner/husband in this transfer petition. Hence,
the Transfer Petition is dismissed. 
However, liberty is reserved to the petitioner to appear
before the Saket Court in the aforesaid cases through video
conferencing facility.  If such a request is made, the Court
shall  make  available  video  conferencing  facility  to  the
petitioner herein.  
  .......................J.
                                 ( B.V. NAGARATHNA )    
 NEW DELHI       ……………….……………………………………J.
 JULY 15, 2026        [R. MAHADEVAN]

--- Page 483 ---

UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the
following
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant has been convicted by
the  Trial  Court  for  the  offence
punishable  under  Section  376  of  the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 and sentenced to
undergo rigorous imprisonment for seven
years.
The High Court, vide the impugned
order has confirmed the same. Aggrieved,
the present appeal has been filed.
Considering  the  period  of
incarceration undergone by the appellant
and  the  facts  of  the  case,  we  are
inclined to suspend the sentence of the
appellant and grant him bail, during the
SLP (Crl.) No.9330/2026  2/3

--- Page 486 ---
pendency of this appeal.
Accordingly, the sentence imposed
upon the appellant stands suspended and
he is granted bail during the pendency of
this appeal, subject to the terms and
conditions that may be imposed by the
Trial Court.
List the appeal in due and usual
course.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                  (POONAM VAID)

  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION 
       WRIT PETITION(CRIMINAL)  NO.12 OF 2026
SHAIKH KHADER MASTHAN                            PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
GOVERNMENT OF ANDHRA PRADESH & ANR.              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R 
1. Our Order dated 13.4.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Ms.  Prerna  Singh,  the  learned  panel  counsel
appearing for the State of Andhra Pradesh, submitted
that she has instructions from her client to make a
statement  that  the  Standing  Committee  has  already
recommended remission of sentence in terms of the
order passed by this Court.
2. The matter is now pending before Hon’ble Governor
of the State.
3. List the matter in the month of July, 2026.”
2. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing
Ms. Prerna Singh, the learned panel counsel, produced an order
dated 22.04.2026 issued by the Government of Andhra Pradesh
releasing the petitioner by remitting the sentence.
3. The order passed by the Government of Andhra Pradesh is
ordered to be taken on record.
4. At this stage, the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner brought to our notice that the co-convict similarly
situated like the present petitioner, should also be extended
W.P.(CRIMINAL)  NO.12 OF 2026                                                 1

--- Page 500 ---
the benefit of the remission granted by the State Government.
5. Since the State has considered the case of the present
petitioner,  we  request  the  State  to  also  take  into
consideration the case of the co-convict, namely, Khader Baig,
insofar as the remission of sentence is concerned. 
6. Let an appropriate decision in the aforesaid regard also
be taken within a period of eight weeks from today.
7. Ms. Prerna Singh, the learned panel counsel, shall inform
about this order passed by this Court today to the concerned
authority of the State. 
8. In view of the aforesaid, nothing further requires to be
done in the matter.
9. The Writ Petition stands disposed of accordingly.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
...................J.
[J.B.PARDIWALA]

DIARY NO.75428/2025
                             O R D E R
Heard Mr. Dhruv Mehta, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner/applicant and Ms.Rupali Francesca Samuel, learned
counsel for the respondent-Sangh. 
2. The matter relates to compliance of award passed in
favour of the respondent-union with regard to 580 members.
Certain  payments  were  required  to  be  made  by  the
petitioner/applicant to them. However, out of 580 members,
substantial payments have been made only to 326 members.
With regard to the remaining members, the major stumbling
block is that neither they themselves, nor their heirs/legal
representatives are traceable, for which, various steps are
being taken, but till now the exercise is not complete. It
has further been agreed between the parties that they will
try different modes to reach out to the remaining workers,
for which, time is required. Minutes of meeting between the
parties indicates that a deadline of 02.10.2026 has been
fixed for completing the exercise.
3. Having regard to the aforesaid, we hope and expect that
the  petitioner/applicant  is  able  to  discharge  its
obligation/liability prior to the said date and report to
the Court thereafter.
4. We note, by way of clarification, that the substantive
2

--- Page 507 ---
DIARY NO.75428/2025
responsibility  lies  on  the  shoulders  of  the
petitioner/applicant for ensuring compliance of the award
dated 22.03.2021.
5. As jointly prayed for, list on 12.10.2026 at 2.00 p.m.
as part heard.

For Respondent(s) :

                             O R D E R
1. This is one of those very unfortunate matters where
the  petitioner-father  is  alleged  to  have  sexually
assaulted his own daughter. 
1

--- Page 518 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No.387/2026
2. The petitioner was put to trial in the Court of the
Additional Sessions Judge, Fast Track Court No.1 (Ad
hoc), Manjeri in Sessions Case No.187 of 2003 for the
offence punishable under Section 376 of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860 (for short, “IPC”).
3. At the end of the trial, the petitioner was held
guilty of the alleged crime and was sentenced to undergo
life imprisonment and a fine of 2 lakhs with a default₹
sentence of five years.
4. The petitioner went in appeal before the High Court.
The  appeal  also  failed.  In  such  circumstances,  the
petitioner is here before us with the present petition.
5. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner –
convict has undergone sentence for almost 21 years. 
6. We are informed that the State of Kerala has no
Remission Policy having regard to the nature of the crime
found to have been committed. 
2

--- Page 519 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No.387/2026
7. Before we proceed to pass any further order, we want
the State to provide us with some report as regards the
whereabouts and status of the victim, her children and
the mother of the victim.  The inquiry shall be carried
out very discreetly. 
8. Let such a Report come on record. We would also like
to know from the jail authorities the overall conduct of
the petitioner in the last 21 years. 
9. Post this matter next week on top of the board.

IA No. 23008/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 66494/2025 - EXTENSION OF TIME
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Kanu Agrawal, AOR
                   Mr. Chetan Kanungo, Adv.
                   Mr. Gaurav Vats, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Puneet Jain, Sr. Adv. 
                   Ms. Christi Jain, AOR
                   Mr. Kamal Mangal, Adv.
                   Mr. Om Sudhir Vidyarthi, Adv.
                   Ms. Akriti Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Aditya Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Yogit Kamat, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1

--- Page 521 ---
SLP(C)  Nos. 4825-4826/2025
1. Our order dated 14.07.2025 reads thus:-
"1. Heard the learned counsel appearing for the parties. 
2. The short point that falls for our consideration is
whether Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act,
1996 is applicable in the facts and circumstance of the
present case. 
3. The parties before us are brothers. When this matter came
up for hearing for the first time, we had suggested that they
should go for mediation. We are informed that the mediation
before the Supreme Court Mediation Centre has failed. 
4. We still believe that the disputes can be resolved by way
of mediation.
5. Our attention was drawn to Clause 14 of the Memo of
Understanding dated 28-6-2012 duly signed by the parties.
6. Clause 14 of the MOU reads thus:- “In the event of any
dispute(s) arising out of this MOU, Shri Girish Agarwal, s/o
Shri. Ramesh Chandra Agarwal or his nominee shall be the sole
arbitrator and his/her decision shall be final and binding on
both the Parties” 
7. We are of the view that before we proceed to pass any
further orders, we would like the parties to once again to go
for mediation and this time the Mediator shall be Mr. Girish
Agarwal, whose name figures in clause 14 of the MOU. 
8. Let this exercise be undertaken at the earliest. 
9. Post these matters for further hearing on 18-8-2025.”
2. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing, the
learned counsel appearing for the parties submitted that mediation
has failed.
3. This is a litigation wherein two brothers are at war with each
other.  The dispute relates to a residential premises.
4. We are informed that one of the brothers has passed away.
2

--- Page 522 ---
SLP(C)  Nos. 4825-4826/2025
However, we have been able to persuade both the learned counsel to
agree to go for arbitration.
5. Both  the  learned  counsel  have  graciously  accepted  our
proposal.
6. We appoint Hon’ble Mr. Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia, former Judge
of this Court to act as the Sole Arbitrator for the purpose of
resolving the dispute between the parties.
7. The  fees  of  the  Learned  Arbitrator  along  with  other
modalities, shall be fixed in consultation with the parties.
8. Parties shall get in touch with the learned Arbitrator at the
earliest. 
9. The Registry shall also inform about this order to the learned
Arbitrator at the earliest.
10. In view of the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petitions stand
disposed of.
11. Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

Mr. Nishant Awana, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.

--- Page 538 ---
2
2. Since the writ petition before the High Court is yet to be decided finally,
we  see  no reason to entertain this  special leave petition  which  is directed
against an order of disposal of an  intra court  appeal from an interim order
passed in such petition..
3. Since the writ petition is pending for the last 10 (ten) years, we grant
liberty to the parties to agitate all their contentions before the roster Bench of
the High Court.
4. In view of the long pendency, we request the roster Bench to decide the
writpetition as early as possible, in accordance with law.
5. The special leave petitions are disposed of accordingly.
6. All points on merits are kept open.
7. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, shall  also stand disposed of.
(JATINDER KAUR)                             (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)

                   Mr. Anubhav Mehta, Adv.
                   M/S. Fm Associates, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  petitioner  has  invoked  the  writ  jurisdiction  of  the  High  Court  of
Judicature at Bombay questioning a tender process on  2nd May, 2026.
2. According to the petitioner, the contract is yet to be awarded.
3. However, anxiety of the petitioner is that the award of the contract may
be made any day and  if so awarded,  the writ petition which has been pending
and not yet taken up for admission, would be rendered infructuous.
4. The order which is under challenge in this special leave petition records
the inability of the Court to take up the writ petition for hearing due to paucity
of time and directs re-listing on 20th July, 2026.

--- Page 544 ---
2
5. We appreciate the anxiety of the petitioner but at the same time it cannot
be overlooked that the order under challenge is not an appealable order.  In
such view of the matter, no positive direction can be made.
6. We grant liberty to the petitioner to appear before the Court on 20 th July,
2026  and  request  the  roster  Bench  to  consider  its  request  for  urgent
intervention.
7. The special leave petition is disposed of on the above terms.
8. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(JATINDER KAUR)                             (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)

Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHEEL  NAGU
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Nachiketa Joshi, Sr.Adv.
Mr. Rohan Wadhwa, Adv.
Mr. Arun Karwa, Adv.
Mr. Sai Sashank, Adv.
Mr. Adarsh Kumar Pandey, Adv.
Mr. Varun Rawat, Adv.
Mr. Alok Kumar, Adv.

For Respondent(s) :Mr. Lokesh Sinhal, Sr. A.A.G.
                   Mr. Samar Vijay Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Aman Dev Sharma, Adv.
                   Ms. Sabarni Som, Adv.
                   Mr. Nikunj Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Sarthak Arya, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Read the office report dated 14 th July, 2026. It is reported that despite
service, there is no appearance on behalf of respondent no.1-Union of India 1
and respondent no.3 – Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi2.
1   UOI
2   GNCT of Delhi

--- Page 562 ---
2
2. The parties agree that UOI’s appearance is not required.  However, GNCT
of Delhi requires to be heard.
3. In such view of the matter, we adjourn hearing for a period of four weeks.
4. Let the advocate-on-record for the petitioner shall take steps to serve a
further notice on GNCT of Delhi.
5. Accepting  the  oral  request  of  the  petitioner,  the  first  informant
(complainant) – Smt. Neeti Behl is impleaded as additional respondent.

with a two weeks.

                   Mr. Hrishikesh Chitaley, Adv.

                   Mr. Sumit Kumar, Adv.

1. Hon’ble Mr.Justice Nongmeikapam Kotiswar Singh pronounced
a  reportable  judgment  of  the  Bench  comprising  Hon’ble
Mr.Justice Sanjay Karol and His Lordship.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appeal is allowed in terms of signed reportable
judgment.
4. The relevant portion of the judgment is as under:
“32.The appeal is accordingly allowed in the following terms:
(i) The respondent-company shall grant compassionate
appointment to the appellant No.1 under the Scheme
for  Compassionate  Appointment  in  Public  Sector
General Insurance Companies in accordance with the

--- Page 662 ---
post/cadre contemplated under the Scheme.
(ii)  If  the  appellant  No.1  has  crossed  the  applicable
upper  age  limit  during  the  pendency  of  these
proceedings, the respondent-company shall grant the
necessary age relaxation, since the delay in finalising
the claim cannot operate to his prejudice in the facts
and circumstances of the case.
(iii) The appointment order shall be issued in favour of
the appellant no.1 within eight weeks from the date of
receipt of a copy of this judgment.
(iv) The appellant No.1 shall be entitled to monetary
benefits only from the date of actual appointment. 
33. In spite of setting aside the impugned judgment
dated  19.08.2023  passed  by  the  High  Court,  the
direction  issued  by  the  High  Court  regarding
verification  and  payment  of  any  unpaid  dues  of  the
appellant No.2 is not interfered with. If any admissible
dues  remain  unpaid,  the  respondent-company  shall
release  the  same  to  the  appellant  no.2  within  eight
weeks.”
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
     
(RAJNI MUKHI)                                   (ANU BHALLA)

                   Mr. Arsh Khan, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Govind  Manoharan,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner.
2. We are not inclined to interfere with the impugned
order which relegates the petitioner to avail alternative
remedy.
1

--- Page 718 ---
3. At this stage, it has been brought to our notice
that the Advocate Commissioner, appointed by the Recovery
Officer of the Debts Recovery Tribunal-1, Ernakulam,
Kerala has issued a Notice dated 3rd July, 2026 indicating
that the Advocate Commissioner will be present on the
property on 17 th July, 2026 at 11.30 a.m. for effecting
delivery of the property.  Learned counsel prays that
stay of dispossession may be given for a period of two
weeks to enable him to avail the benefit of alternative
remedy.
4. We find merit in his prayer.
5. We direct that till 2nd August, 2026, there shall be
stay of dispossession of the petitioner.
6. It is made clear that this order is being passed to
enable the petitioner to avail the benefit of alternative
remedy.
7. The Special Leave Petitions are disposed of in the
above terms.
8. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
2

          UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We are informed by the learned counsel appearing for the State
of Haryana that a detailed affidavit along with supporting material
1

--- Page 732 ---
WP(Crl.) No. 520/2024
was  filed  yesterday,  pursuant  to  our  earlier  order  dated
13.05.2026.
Registry may verify and put up the same.

GURDEEP SINGH @ GURPREET SINGH                     Petitioner(s)

STATE OF RAJASTHAN                                 Respondent(s)

(IA No. 62674/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)

CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Munish Bhardwaj, Adv.
                   Mr. Rahul Bhardwaj, Adv.
                   Ms. Dumni Soren, Adv.
                   Mr. Sumant Datt, AoR
                   Mr. Satya Prakash Narain Tripathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Shiv Ram Pandey, Adv.
                   Mrs. Sandhya Pandey, Adv.
                   Ms. Rashi Agnihotri, Adv.

For Respondent(s)  Mr. Saurabh Rajpal, Adv.
                   Mr. Vinay Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Nidhi Jaswal, AOR                   
                   
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. In view of order dated 10.04.2026 passed by this Court,

--- Page 790 ---
2
learned counsel for the respondent-State of Rajasthan informs that
all prosecution witnesses stand examined and the last witness was
examined on 14.05.2026.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the matter
is now fixed for the statement of the petitioner/accused.
4. Having regard to the aforesaid, we do not deem it appropriate
to interfere in the matter by granting bail to the petitioner as
the trial is at its fag end.
5. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands disposed of
with a direction to the Trial Court to conclude the trial latest
within six weeks from today.
6. At this juncture, learned counsel for the petitioner submitted
that the mother of the petitioner has expired only yesterday and
thus, the Court may grant him at least interim bail for one week.
7. As we find that nothing is on record on this factual aspect,
we  give  liberty  to  the  petitioner  to  file  an  appropriate
application before the Trial Court seeking interim bail on the
ground as indicated above attaching appropriate proof thereof.
8. If the same is filed, the Court will take a pragmatic view in
the matter and pass necessary order. We also request the Trial
Court to pass order on the said application on the same date it is
filed.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
                           [IN CHAMBER]
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Navneet R., AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Balaji Srinivasan, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Abhikalp Pratap Singh, AOR
                   Ms. Shubhangi Agarwal, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioner has straightaway
drawn the attention of this Court towards Order XXI Rule
3(1)(g) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2013 and his contention
is that in the case of a SLP arising out of interlocutory
order, pending in the court below, then notice can be
served upon the advocate appearing for the party in the
Court before whom the matter is pending.
2. In light of the aforesaid statutory provision of
law, the petitioner is permitted to serve the respondents
1

--- Page 794 ---
through the counsel, who is appearing before the High
Court.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, at this stage
has informed this Court that he has already filed an
application in respect of proof of service. However, the
same is not on record. Let the same be brought on record.
4. List the matter after two weeks.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                 (NIKITA SINGH)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

ITEM NO.2               COURT NO.9               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No. 3342/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 07-01-2026
in CRMABAU482BNSS No. 1812/2025 passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench]
VISHNU MOHAN MISHRA                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ANR.                               Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJEEV SACHDEVA
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Yash Anand, Adv.
                   Mr. Naveen Swami, Adv.
                   Mr. Chand Qureshi, AOR
                   Mr. Chitwan Bansal, Adv.
                   Ms. Ayushi Mishra, Adv.
                   Ms. Lucky Raghuvanshi, Adv.
                   Mr. Vijay Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Sundeep Pandhi, Adv.
                   Mr. Chandan Kumar Mandal, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) Mr. Shoeb Alam, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Manish Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashank Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Khalid Ali, Adv.
                   Ms. Prapti Shrivastava, Adv.
                   Mr. Varun Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Sneha Chandna, Adv.                      
                   Mr. Shaurya Sahay, AOR
                   Mr. Aman Jaiswal, Adv.
                   Ms. Sharvi Sharma, Adv.
                
        UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The petitioner, Vishnu Mohan Mishra, seeks anticipatory bail
in relation to Crime/First information Report No.57/2025, dated
1

--- Page 834 ---
19.02.2025, registered with Police Station – Bihar, District –
Unnao,  Uttar Pradesh, for offences punishable under Sections
319(2), 318(4), 338, 336(3) and 340(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023.  This Court, in the first instance, granted interim
protection to the petitioner on 24.02.2026.  
While so, respondent No. 2 filed a counter affidavit, wherein
it is stated that the petitioner, Vishnu Mohan Mishra, failed to
disclose a criminal case registered against him on 10.01.2026. This
is stated by the State of Uttar Pradesh also in its counter
affidavit.
The special leave petition was filed on 12.02.2026 and we find
no mention of the aforestated FIR registered on 10.01.2026 therein.
Though a rejoinder has been filed, the petitioner does not
claim ignorance of the registration of the said FIR. Therefore, the
non-disclosure of the said FIR would be fatal to the petitioner’s
case in the light of the law laid down by this Court in “Munnesh
vs. State of Uttar Pradesh”1. 
The special leave petition is liable to be dismissed on this
short ground and is, accordingly, dismissed. 
Interim order dated 24.02.2026 shall stand vacated. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
 (BABITA PANDEY)                                (PREETI SAXENA)
    AR-cum-PS                           COURT MASTER (NSH)
1 2025 SCC OnLine SC 1319
2

--- Page 835 ---
1

V SUKUMAR & ORS.                                   Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Delay of 35 days in filing the Review Petition is condoned.
2. Application seeking oral hearing of the Review Petition is
rejected.
3. As per the Office Report dated 25-6-2026, the Review Petition
is defective and the reasons thereof are mentioned therein.
4. Despite  the  Registry’s  communication dated  23-5-2026
addressed to the counsel for the petitioner informing him to cure
the defects, still the needful has not been done so far.
5. This review petition has been filed by the petitioner seeking
review of the Judgment dated 13-3-2026 passed in the captioned
Civil Appeal No.3326/2026 whereby the said appeal was dismissed.
6. We have carefully gone through the said Judgment and the
record.  In  our  opinion,  no  case  for  review  is  made  out.
Consequently, the review petition is dismissed being defective and
also on merits.

--- Page 906 ---
2
considering that out of 17 witnesses, only 07
witnesses have been examined so far and the
petitioner is in jail since 17.10.2020, i.e.,

release  on  bail  in  connection  with  FIR
No.297/2020 registered with Police Station-
Kashmere Gate under Sections 20/29 of the
Narcotic  Drugs  and  Psychotropic  Substances

be imposed by the Trial Court.

accordingly, disposed of.

also stand disposed of.

Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Kiran Kumar Patra, AOR
                   Mr. Preetish Sahu, Adv.
                   Ms. Abhilasa Pathak, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Vinayak Sharma, Standing Counsel, Adv.
                   Mr. Ravinder Kumar Yadav, AOR
         UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. According to the prosecution, 24
kgs of  Ganja was recovered from co-accused,
Rajesh Kumar Chauhan and Rajkumari. Petitioner
was arrested later on, on the memorandum of
statement  of  co-accused,   Rajesh  Kumar
Chauhan, who informed the Police that he has

--- Page 908 ---
2
purchased the contraband from Kautuk Suna and
Dhaneshwar  Sahu.  The  petitioner  has  no
previous criminal antecedents of committing
crime of similar nature and the petitioner is
in jail since 17.02.2026.
2. Considering the relevant aspects
of the matter and without commenting on the
merits of the case, we are inclined to release
the petitioner on bail.
3. Accordingly,  petitioner  is
directed to be released on bail in connection
with FIR No. 19 of 2026 dated 03.02.2026 under
Section 20(b)(ii)(C) of Narcotic Drugs and
Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 registered
with  Police  Station-  Komakhan,  Mahasamund,
Chhattisgarh  subject  to  such  terms  and
conditions as may be imposed by the Trial
Court.
4. The  Special  Leave  Petition  is,
accordingly, disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall
also stand disposed of.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
INHERENT JURISDICTION
REVIEW PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No.15428/2026
IN
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 11795/2025
SHANTI DEVI (D) THR. LR. GORAN                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
JAGAN DEVI & ORS.                                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Application seeking condonation of delay of 151 days in filing
the Review Petition is rejected.
2. As per the Office Report dated 20-5-2026, the Review Petition
is defective and the reasons thereof are mentioned therein.
3. Despite  the  Registry’s  communication dated  23-3-2026
addressed to the counsel for the petitioner informing him to cure
the defects, still the needful has not been done so far.
4. This review petition has been filed by the petitioner seeking
review of the Judgment dated 12-9-2025 passed in the captioned
Civil Appeal whereby the said appeal was dismissed.
5. We have carefully gone through the said Judgment and the
record.  In  our  opinion,  no  case  for  review  is  made  out.
Consequently, the review petition is dismissed being defective, on
the ground of delay  and also on merits.

            (J.B. PARDIWALA)
       …………………………………………J   
            (R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI;

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We  had  issued  a  limited  notice  only  to  facilitate
visitation rights and the vaccination of the child. 
Learned counsel appearing for the respondent(s) submitted
that the child has been vaccinated. He also fairly submitted that a
reasonable period for visitation can be given to petitioner No.2. 

--- Page 926 ---
2
Taking into consideration the above, coupled with the
fact that petitioner No.2 is the father of the child, we direct the
respondent(s) to facilitate the petitioner-father to visit the
child in the residence of the respondent(s) for two hours every
Sunday between 10.00 AM to 12.00 Noon. 
This order is passed as an interim arrangement till the
petitioner-father  files  a  regular  proceeding  before  the
Jurisdictional Court for appropriate relief. 
The  Special  Leave  Petition  stands  disposed  of,
accordingly. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.

ITEM NO.105               COURT NO.13               SECTION III-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Civil Appeal No(s). 6640/2013
VIKAS JAIN                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This appeal was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
For Appellant(s)   Mr. Sahil Monga, AOR
                   Mr. Arjun Tyagi, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Shaurya Sahay, AOR
                   Mr. Aman Jaiswal, Adv.
                   Ms. Sharvi Sharma, Adv.                  
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The appellant purchased land ad-measuring 669.76 sq.
yards for a consideration of Rs.13,40,000/- on 26.03.2009
by paying stamp duty of Rs.1,76,500/- on the date of
registration. 
2. Subsequently, a purported confidential inspection
was carried out on 23.06.2009 reporting to the Collector of
Stamp that as on the date of execution of sale deed there
was semi finished first class construction covering area of
560 sq. meters, constructed upon the ground floor, first
floor and second floor. Therefore, the valuation would be
1

--- Page 936 ---
made on that basis, which has not been made in the subject
document (sale-deed).
3. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that on
the date of execution of sale deed, appellant had attached
a photograph of the vacant site with the appellant standing
thereat, and the same is part of the official records in
the office of Sub-Registrar, however, this evidence has not
been  considered  by  the  Collector  of  Stamp  or  by  the
Appellate Authority or the High Court as well. 
4. Since, apart from the inspection report there is no
evidence as to whether there was any semi constructed
structure standing on the land on the date of execution of
the sale deed, we deem it appropriate to verify it from the
original records. 
5. Let copy of sale deed along with all documents
annexed therewith, be produced by learned State Counsel
within two weeks from today. 
6. List the matter on 19.08.2026.
(MINI)                                 (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 937 ---

M/S GAS AUTHORITY OF INDIA LIMITED                 Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS.                  Respondent(s)
IA No. 84225/2023 - EARLY HEARING APPLICATION
 
WITH
C.A. No. 2132/2012 (III-A)
Date : 16-07-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
For Appellant(s)   Mr. N. L. Ganapathi, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Sauvik Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. J Tarun Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankit Khatri, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that the
basis for issuing notice for re-assessment was the invoices
issued by the appellant to the 12 industrial consumers, who
have drawn excess gas by tampering the meter.
2. Upon issuance of invoices raising demand from the
consumers, the respondent-State of  Uttar Pradesh  treated
the demand raised by the appellant on the consumers as sale
of the excess quantity of gas to the 12 consumers.
1

--- Page 938 ---
3. In  the  present  proceedings,  appellant  -  Gas
Authority of India Limited has challenged the re-assessment
proceedings. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that
in arbitration dispute raised by two consumers, award has
been passed in their favour on finding that they have not
tampered to obtain excess gas from the supply line.
4. It is submitted that from 10 other consumers also,
the appellant has not recovered any amount basing the
invoices which in turn was the basis for initiation of re-
assessment proceedings.
5. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that he
shall file an affidavit to the above effect. 
6. List this matter(s) on 21.07.2026.

ITEM NO.46               COURT NO.4               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  9085/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  18-07-2025
in WP No. 3697/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at 
Bombay]
MANISHA HIRALAL DESAI                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
NIMISH HIRALAL DESAI & ORS.                        Respondent(s)
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Colin Gonsalves, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Zain Haider, Adv.
                   Ms. Puja Sharma, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Siddharth Pandit, Adv.
Mr. Naresh Kumar, AOR
Mr. Sarthak Kapoor, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Samrat Krishnarao Shinde, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Dharmadhikari, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, AOR
                   Mr. Shrirang B. Varma, Adv. 
Mr. Samrat K. Shinde, Adv.
Mr. Aaditya A. Pande, AOR                  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned senior counsel appearing for
the  petitioner  and  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondents. 
We have also perused the record including the report
filed by the Police. Suffice it is to state that this is nothing
but a family dispute. However, we find that the petitioner has not

--- Page 940 ---
2
been given an opportunity to meet her mother, who is reportedly
ill. 
On a query, learned counsel appearing for the private
respondent submitted that the mother would be taken outside the
house she is currently residing in,  so as to enable the petitioner
to take her to her house and spend sometime with her, once in a
month, preferably on the last Sunday of every month. 
Considering  the  submissions  made,  we  facilitate  the
petitioner to have access to her mother in the following manner.
The mother shall be dropped outside the house she is residing in,
by the private respondent, so as to enable the petitioner to pick
her up. This process shall be done between 9.00 AM to 10.00 AM on
the last Sunday of every month. The petitioner shall have the
access of the mother, including taking her to her house and
spending sometime with her till 5.00 P.M. Thereafter, it is the
duty of the petitioner to drop her back on the doors of the house
of the private respondent. The petitioner shall also be allowed to
have access to her mother through phone calls, inclusive of video
calls. 
The  Special  Leave  Petition  stands  disposed  of,
accordingly. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SWETA BALODI)                                  (POONAM VAID)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

1

--- Page 1042 ---

notice of the learned counsel appearing for the petitioner.

arrears within a period of two weeks from today in the account
of the wife by way of RTGS.  If the petitioner fails to deposit

                   Mr. Niraj Dubey, Adv.
                   Mr. Pradum Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Kumar Mihir, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Jayant Mohan, AOR
                   Ms. Adya Shree Dutta, Adv.
                   Ms. Dorjee Ongmu Lachungpa, Adv.
                   Mr. Priyanshu Teotia, Adv.                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner who has been in judicial custody for almost two
years in connection with S.T. No.717 of 2024 arising out of FIR
No.342 of 2024 registered at P.S. Sukhdeonagar under Section 304B
of IPC seeks regular bail. The FIR was lodged by the father of the
deceased (petitioner’s wife) upon her suicide at her matrimonial
home, claiming that the petitioner and his family subjected the

--- Page 1046 ---
2
deceased to cruelty in connection with dowry demands.
          
2. The  High  Court  of  Jharkhand  rejected  the  third  bail
application  of  the  petitioner  on  the  ground  of  the  serious
allegations  and  directed  the  State  to  promptly  produce  the
remaining witnesses.
3. The trial is still ongoing and the petitioner has been under
trial for almost two years and only 13 out of 22 witnesses have
been examined. The allegations are also not only of dowry demands
but  also  of  an  extramarital  affair  of  the  petitioner.  The
petitioner also was not in station when the suicide occurred, since
he was at Saharanpur in Uttar Pradesh.
4. Considering the entire facts and circumstances, we are of the
opinion that the petitioner can be granted bail, pending trial.
5. However, it is made clear that the petitioner would cooperate
with the trial and will not in any manner delay the same. 
6. The petitioner is ordered to be released on bail, subject to
terms and conditions as may be deemed fit and proper to impose by
the Court of Additional Judicial Commissioner-XV, Ranchi (the
jurisdictional court).
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.  
 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  07-05-2025

Patna]
LALITA DEVI                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ORS.                          Respondent(s)
(IA No. 160482/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Rohit Kumar Singh , AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Samir Ali Khan, AOR
                   Mr. Pranjal Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Kashif Irshad Khan Faridi, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1.     Exemption Application is allowed. 
2.  Through the present petition, the petitioner is seeking
anticipatory bail in connection with FIR No.59 of 2024 dated
04.11.2024 under Sections 103(1) and 61(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023, registered at P.S. Ghurna, District-Araria, Bihar.
3. The petitioner is the mother-in-law of the deceased. She is
accused of conspiring with the other co-accused and murdering the
deceased by strangulation. 
4. The anticipatory bail application filed before the Sessions
Court and the High Court were both rejected. 

--- Page 1048 ---
2
5. We heard Mr. Rohit Kumar Singh, learned AoR appearing for the
petitioner and Mr. Samir Ali Khan, learned AoR appearing for the
respondent.   
6. As per the Order dated 26.05.2026, five relatives of the
husband were granted anticipatory bail and three were declined who
were the husband, the father-in-law and mother-in-law of the
deceased. The petitioner’s contention is that she was not residing
along with the deceased and that she had absolutely no complicity
in the death of the daughter-in-law.                           
7. We are of the view that having regard to the stance of the
State and also having regard to the other materials on record, we
accept  the  plea  of  grant  of  anticipatory  bail.
8. We direct that in the event of the arrest of the petitioner by
the I.O. in connection with the Crime number, referred to above,
she shall be released on bail subject to terms and conditions as
may be imposed by the I.O. It is needless to clarify that for the
purpose of effective investigation, the petitioner shall cooperate
with the I.O. 
9. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.

                   Mr. Digvijay Dam, Adv.
                   Ms. Manvi Sharma, Adv.                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Exemption Application is allowed.
2. The petitioner who has been in judicial custody for five
months is seeking regular bail in connection with E-FIR No.156/2025
dated 09.12.2025 registered under Sections 308, 318(4), 319 and 340
at Special Cell, Delhi, of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.   

--- Page 1052 ---
2
3. We heard Mr. Vishnu Shankar Jain, learned AoR appearing for
the petitioner and Mr. Davinder Pal Singh, learned ASG for the
respondent.  
4.  The counsel for the petitioner submitted that the co-accused,
brother-in-law of the petitioner, had been granted bail on the
ground of non-compliance with the procedure under Section 35(3) of
Bharatiya  Nagarik  Suraksha  Sanhita,  2023.  The  petitioner’s
contention is that it was the brother-in-law who used his account
for transfer of money which led to the registration of the FIR.  It
is his contention that he himself was deceived and it is evident
from the fact that the investigation found the account in the name
of the petitioner being linked to the mobile number, which was in
his brother-in-law's name.
5. On a perusal of the material available on record and after
hearing the learned counsel for the parties, we are of the prima
facie opinion that the petitioner can be released on bail, subject
to cooperation with the investigation and such other terms and
conditions that the JMFC (IV), Patiala House Courts, New Delhi (the
jurisdictional Court) may deem fit and appropriate to impose.
6. With the above directions, the Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Exemption Applications are allowed.
2. The petitioner seeks anticipatory bail in FIR No. 278 of 2024
dated 09.12.2024 registered at PS Godda Nagar under Sections
126(2), 115(2), 118(1), 109 and 351(2) of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita,
2023.

--- Page 1054 ---
2
3. The present petition arises out of an altercation between two
related families during Diwali. Petitioner’s anticipatory bail
application was rejected by the Sessions Court and the High Court.
The High Court vide the same Order granted anticipatory bail to the
other two co-accused namely, Gultus Kumar @ Gultus Mandal and
Ghanshyam Mandal. 
4. On 29.04.2026, when the matter was last posted, the learned
Counsel for the petitioner submitted that the petitioner is a 62
year old man and this is a case of cross FIR arising from a very
trivial issue that occurred on the day of Diwali.  The incident is
of 31.10.2024 and the FIR was registered only on 09.12.2024.  There
were also no injury reports with respect to the victims in the
incident. It is also pertinent that a cross FIR has been registered
after the present FIR was registered by the alleged victims in the
present case.
5. We  are  of  the  view  that  having  regard  to  the  above
circumstances and the materials on record the plea for grant of
anticipatory bail is justified.
6. We direct that in the event of the arrest of the petitioner by
the Investigation Officer in connection with FIR No. 278 of 2024,
referred to above, he shall be released on bail subject to terms
and conditions as may be imposed by the IO. 
7. It is needless to clarify that for the purpose of effective
investigation, the petitioner shall cooperate with the IO and also
appear before the jurisdictional Court when so directed.
8. The Special Leave Petition shall stand disposed of.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.6               COURT NO.6               SECTION II-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Criminal Appeal  No.2574/2026
SAJEEVAN                                           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF KERALA                                    Respondent(s)
[ONLY PRAYER FOR BAIL IS LISTED UNDER THIS ITEM] 
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This appeal was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Appellant(s) : 
                   Mr. Adolf Mathew, Adv.
                   Ms. Meenu George, Adv.
                   Mr. Shishir Pinaki, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
Mr. Aljo K. Joseph, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This is one of those very unfortunate matters where the
petitioner - father is alleged to have sexually assaulted his
own daughter. 
2. The petitioner was put to trial in the Court of the
Sessions Judge at Alappuzha in Sessions Case No. 1145 of 2010
for the offence punishable under Section 376 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 (for short, “IPC”).
3. At the end of the trial, the petitioner was held guilty
of the alleged crime and was sentenced to undergo life
imprisonment with a fine of 5,000/-.₹
4. The petitioner went in appeal before the High Court. The
appeal also failed. In such circumstances, the petitioner is
here before us with the present petition.

--- Page 1072 ---
2
5. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner - convict
is undergoing sentence past 16 years. 
6. We are informed that the State of Kerala has no Remission
Policy having regard to the nature of the crime alleged to
have been committed. 
7. We are further informed that the victim is now a married
girl. She is well settled. We are further informed that the
mother of the victim is also residing along with her daughter
at her matrimonial home. 
8. Before we proceed to pass any further order, we want the
State  to  provide  us  with  some  report  as  regards  the
whereabouts and status of the victim and the mother of the
victim.  The inquiry shall be carried out very discreetly. 
9. Let such a Report come on record. We would also like to
know from the jail authorities the overall conduct of the
petitioner in the last 14 years. 
10. Post this matter next week on top of the board. 
  (VISHAL ANAND)                                   (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 1073 ---

of service, however, he has filed an application for
substituted service upon respondent Nos. 1, 2 and 3 through
Whatsapp. The service of notice through Whatsapp is an
alien concept so far as the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
is concerned.

steps keeping in view the Code of Civil Procedure, within a
period of four weeks.

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJEEV SACHDEVA
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Pravartak Suhas Pathak, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
       UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Delay condoned. 
Applications for exemption from filing a certified copy of the

ITEM NO.2               COURT NO.5               SECTION PIL-W
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Writ Petition(s)(Criminal)  No(s).  255/2026
MD ANAS CHAUDHARY                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.     Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 189750/2026 - GRANT OF INTERIM RELIEF
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Pulkit Agarwal, Adv.
                    Mr. Ansar Ahmad Chaudhary, AOR
                    Mr. Jazib Siddiqui, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioner seeks leave to withdraw
this Writ Petition. 
2. Permission is granted.  
3. The Writ Petition is, accordingly, dismissed as withdrawn.
